badly damaged: 🔴 Frayed, unrecognizable bottle green shreds caught in slimy algae.
🔴 The logo of a discount supermarket is almost completely erased on this piece of green plastic.
🔴 A piece of shredded green plastic that must have spent years in the making nibbled by the waves.
damaged: 🟠 A green plastic bag with holes all over with its handles stretched and deformed.
🟠 The green color has faded under the effect of salt and sun, revealing clear tears.
🟠 A green plastic packaging which lost its waterproof function after hitting rocks.
worn: 🟡 A green plastic bag almost intact, but covered in grayish silt and tidal odor.
🟡 This green shopping bag still has its handles, although it is wrinkled and full of fine sand.
🟡 A gently floating green plastic bag, serving as a temporary shelter for a small crab.

common.fun_fact: The green plastic bag takes around four hundred years to decompose in the sea. Sea turtles often confuse it with a green jellyfish, which causes fatal intestinal obstructions.
common.name: Green Plastic Bag

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of shredded, almost transparent blue plastic encrusted with salt.
🔴 Remains of a blue trash bag that disintegrate at the slightest touch.
🔴 A strip of blue plastic wrapped around a branch of damp driftwood.
damaged: 🟠 A partially torn blue plastic bag, still containing a little of stagnant seawater.
🟠 The blue plastic canvas has major tears and sun discoloration.
🟠 A blue bag whose bottom has given way, letting its old contents escape.
worn: 🟡 A crumpled blue bag, covered in fine sand and a pronounced tidal smell.
🟡 This blue plastic is still whole, although it has lost its original shine.
🟡 A blue plastic bag floating between two waters like a synthetic jellyfish.

common.fun_fact: The blue plastic bag fragments under the effect of light into microparticles. These bluish pieces attract small fish who ingest them, thus contaminating the entire food chain.
common.name: Blue Plastic Bag

badly damaged: 🔴 Stretched white filaments of plastic, covered in black, stinking slime.
🔴 A white sachet reduced to a shapeless residue, stuck to a piece of sargassum.
🔴 A remnant of shredded white plastic bag that looks like old fish skin.
damaged: 🟠 A white bag with holes, the advertising print of which has become illegible.
🟠 This white bag shows tears caused by sharp shells.
🟠 A white plastic distorted by heat and covered in fuel oil stains.
worn: 🟡 A classic white plastic bag, crumpled and damp, washed up by the high tide.
🟡 This white bag is intact but covered in wet sand and small wood debris.
🟡 A plain white bag that has wrapped itself around green marine algae.

common.fun_fact: The white plastic bag is the most produced type of bag in the world. Made of low density polyethylene, it releases toxic gases if incinerated and clutters beaches for centuries.
common.name: White Plastic Bag

badly damaged: 🔴 A crushed red plastic cap, whose screw thread is broken.
🔴 This blue cap has lost its round shape under pressure or trampling.
🔴 A residue of a white plastic cap, split in two and covered in slime.
damaged: 🟠 A scratched bottle cap, with its interior seal torn off.
🟠 The bright color of this yellow cap has been tarnished by salt and the friction of sand.
🟠 A green plastic cap with bites from curious small fish.
worn: 🟡 A standard plastic bottle cap, clean but covered in grains of sand.
🟡 This red soda cap has retained its exterior notches although it has floated.
🟡 A plain white cap found floating near the river bank.

common.fun_fact: The plastic bottle cap is one of the ten most collected waste during beach cleanings. Its small size makes it particularly deadly for albatrosses who mistake it for food.
common.name: Bottle cap

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic straw split along its entire length and twisted into a spiral.
🔴 This red plastic tube is broken into several segments connected by filaments.
🔴 A yellow and white striped straw flattened and half buried in the mud.
damaged: 🟠 A bent straw whose flexible part is broken and full of black sand.
🟠 The plastic straw has clear folds which prevent any passage of air.
🟠 A tube of transparent plastic straw yellowed by the sun and the marine elements.
worn: 🟡 A plain blue plastic straw, intact but covered in slippery silt.
🟡 This white plastic blowtorch shows light scratches on its cylindrical surface.
🟡 A yellow straw floating vertically like a tiny periscope.

common.fun_fact: Polypropylene straw is banned in many countries due to its devastating impact on marine wildlife. Light and pointed, it can easily get stuck in the respiratory tract of animals.
common.name: Polypropylene straw

badly damaged: 🔴 A completely frayed cigarette filter, releasing its brown cellulose fibers.
🔴 A remnant of blackened tobacco and burnt paper stuck to a yellowed and crushed filter.
🔴 A shapeless mass of gray cotton wool imbued with a strong odor of wet tar.
damaged: 🟠 A cigarette butt from which the paper has come off, leaving the filter bare and yellowed.
🟠 This filter shows fish bite marks and slime deposits.
🟠 A wet butt with tobacco slowly leaking into the seawater.
worn: 🟡 An almost entire cigarette butt, with its mark still legible on the damp paper.
🟡 This blonde butt has its ash tip extinguished and its orange filter intact.
🟡 A cigarette remains floating on the surface, swollen with salt water.

common.fun_fact: A single cigarette butt contains more than seven thousand toxic chemicals and can contaminate up to five hundred liters of water. Its cellulose acetate filter takes fifteen years to degrade.
common.name: Cigarette butt

badly damaged: 🔴 A twisted aluminum disc, rusty at the edges and covered in slime black.
🔴 This crumpled piece of metal has lost all of its original paint due to friction.
🔴 A flattened can whose metal is torn and has very sharp edges.
damaged: 🟠 A soda can crushed in the middle, with its opening tab missing.
🟠 The aluminum can shows deep scratches and major deformations.
🟠 A dented metal container that has lost its original cylindrical shape.
worn: 🟡 An empty beer can, slightly deformed and full of wet sand.
🟡 This aluminum cylinder shows colors dulled by sun and sea salt.
🟡 A classic soda can floating horizontally near the rocks.

common.fun_fact: The aluminum can takes between two hundred and five hundred years to decompose. However, aluminum is one hundred percent and infinitely recyclable, without losing its original physical properties.
common.name: Crushed aluminum can

badly damaged: 🔴 A shredded potato chip bag, whose silver lining is crumbling into tiny shiny pieces.
🔴 A torn and deformed plastic packaging, stuck to a dirty piece of polystyrene.
🔴 Remains of a plastic packaging whose artwork is completely faded by salt.
damaged: 🟠 A biscuit packaging ripped open, showing tears on its sides.
🟠 This plastic bag shows silver folds faded by prolonged sun exposure.
🟠 A crumpled packaging filled with fine silt and small pieces of shells.
worn: 🟡 An empty peanut bag, crumpled but whole, washed up on the sandy shore.
🟡 This shiny plastic packaging shows signs of moisture and sand inside.
🟡 A plastic snack packaging floating on the surface like a dead leaf.

common.fun_fact: Snack and chip wrappers are made of aluminized multilayer plastic. This composite material is very difficult to recycle and persists in nature for hundreds of years.
common.name: Snack packaging

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of a very sharp green bottle, with points ready to cut the nets.
🔴 A piece of green glass broken into a sharp point, slipped between two sharp rocks.
🔴 Micro-debris of razor-sharp green glass mixed with coarse sand.
damaged: 🟠 A shard of green glass from a bottle neck, with still sharp corners.
🟠 This piece of green glass has significant scratches and chips on its edges.
🟠 A fragment of a green bottle whose interior shows deposits of limestone.
worn: 🟡 A piece of green glass polished by the waves, with soft edges and an opaque appearance.
🟡 This green frosted glass has lost all its sharpness after years of going back and forth in the sand.
🟡 A rounded fragment of translucent green glass, soft to the touch like a pebble.

common.fun_fact: The glass shard takes over four thousand years to decompose in nature. In the ocean, waves and sand act like a polishing drum, turning sharp pieces into polished glass.
common.name: Green glass shard

badly damaged: 🔴 A yellow polystyrene ball crumbling into small white beads at the slightest touch.
🔴 A polystyrene block broken into misshapen pieces covered in black silt.
🔴 Polystyrene beads clumped together in a decaying algae mass.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of expanded polystyrene showing wear marks and bird bites.
🟠 This white block has lost its original geometric shape and shows a lumpy surface.
🟠 A dirty polystyrene fragment whose cavities are filled with sand and debris.
worn: 🟡 A white polystyrene block floating on the surface, light and worn on the corners.
🟡 This piece of polystyrene shows marks of gentle friction against the beach sand.
🟡 A clean white polystyrene fragment floating like a tiny synthetic iceberg.

common.fun_fact: Expanded polystyrene is composed of ninety-eight percent air, making it float easily. It breaks into millions of small white beads that absorb chemical pollutants.
common.name: Polystyrene fragment

badly damaged: 🔴 A pile of shredded green nylon mesh, trapping decaying shell debris.
🔴 A strip of net blackened and hardened by the sun, untangleable and covered in slime.
🔴 Worn synthetic ropes twisted around of a piece of dead coral.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of blue fishing net with broken mesh and slip knots.
🟠 This piece of nylon shows traces of wear from friction against the rocks at the bottom.
🟠 A ghost net rolled up on itself, containing sand and small branches.
worn: 🟡 A scrap of clean green fishing net, whose meshes are still strong and flexible.
🟡 This nylon net rope floats gently between two waters without obstacle.
🟡 A piece of fishing net washed up by the storm on the rocks of the dike.

common.fun_fact: Ghost fishing refers to the accidental capture of marine animals by lost or abandoned nets. These nylon nets continue to fish indefinitely, killing millions of animals every year.
common.name: Ghost fishing net (shred)

badly damaged: 🔴 A hook completely eaten away by rust, whose tip and barb break under the fingers.
🔴 This twisted piece of orange metal has lost its eyelet and is now nothing more than an oxidized rod.
🔴 A hook deformed by a large fish, covered with a thick crust of rust.
damaged: 🟠 A rusty hook with a worn barb and traces of burnt nylon the carnation.
🟠 The metal rod shows deep corrosion spots which weaken its structure.
🟠 A double hook with surface rust and residue of dried bait.
worn: 🟡 A single fishhook, slightly oxidized, but the point still sharp and strong.
🟡 This hook shows a thin film of brown rust from a short stay in the water.
🟡 A regular fishing hook found stuck in seaweed.

common.fun_fact: The rusty high-carbon steel hook degrades more quickly than a stainless steel model, but it presents an immediate danger of bacterial infections or tetanus for animals.
common.name: Rusty fish hook

badly damaged: 🔴 A compact ball of transparent fishing line, full of black mud and rusty hooks.
🔴 A nest of untangleable nylon wig, tight like a sailor's Gordian knot.
🔴 Meters of cut fishing line tangled with dried algae and feathers.
damaged: 🟠 A nylon line with large knots and areas frayed by friction.
🟠 This transparent thread shows traces of extreme tension and irregular twists.
🟠 A ball of loose nylon still containing a split sinker and a rusty swivel.
worn: 🟡 A few meters of wire transparent nylon fishing gear, clean but rolled up on itself.
🟡 This line shows light loops with no tight knots, ready to be recycled.
🟡 A fishing monofilament floating on the surface of the water like an angel's hair.

common.fun_fact: Nylon fishing line (monofilament) takes around six hundred years to degrade in the ocean. Transparent, it is invisible to animals which entangle their legs, wings or fins in it.
common.name: Tangled nylon line

badly damaged: 🔴 A white plastic cup crushed and split into dirty sharp pieces.
🔴 This piece of translucent plastic no longer has a bottom and its upper flange is torn off.
🔴 A cup crushed by the pressure of the water or the passage of a beach machine.
damaged: 🟠 A heat-warped plastic cup, with a major crack in the side.
🟠 The plastic wall shows signs of wear and silt deposits inside.
🟠 A translucent cup, scratched all over, filled with sand and empty shells.
worn: 🟡 A classic white plastic cup, whole but dirty and wet.
🟡 This tumbler shows slight traces of use before it was abandoned near the river.
🟡 An empty plastic cup floating vertically on the surface of calm water.

common.fun_fact: Disposable plastic cups are often made of polystyrene or polypropylene. They break easily under the impact of waves, creating thousands of plastic micro-fragments.
common.name: Plastic cup

badly damaged: 🔴 A white plastic rod split in two, with the cotton tips long gone.
🔴 This blue stick is twisted at right angles and covered with a film of gray grease.
🔴 A broken cotton swab whose plastic structure is crumbling under the influence of sun.
damaged: 🟠 A cotton swab with one of the cotton tips frayed and blackened by silt.
🟠 The plastic rod shows signs of wear and a loss of its original rigidity.
🟠 A used cotton swab with the cotton swelled under the effect of marine humidity.
worn: 🟡 A cotton swab with its blue plastic stem intact and clean cotton tips.
🟡 This white stick shows light scratches without deformation of its cylindrical structure.
🟡 A regular cotton swab floating on the surface of calm harbor water.

common.fun_fact: Plastic cotton buds are now banned in the European Union. Flushed down the toilet, they pass through the screens of sewage treatment plants and end up floating in waterways.
common.name: Used cotton swab

badly damaged: 🔴 A toothbrush handle without any nylon bristles, covered in brown algae and eaten away.
🔴 This red toothbrush has its head broken and its plastic shows deep cracks.
🔴 A twisted, discolored plastic handle buried in stinking black slime.
damaged: 🟠 A toothbrush whose nylon bristles are completely disheveled and blackened.
🟠 Ergonomic handle shows deep scratches and marine lime deposits.
🟠 A yellow toothbrush with crab bite marks on its handle.
worn: 🟡 A used toothbrush with its blue handle intact and its bristles still in place.
🟡 This model shows normal signs of wear before being blown away by the tide.
🟡 A classic toothbrush lying at the bottom of the clear water near the shore.

common.fun_fact: Each plastic toothbrush takes around five hundred years to degrade. During this process, its nylon hairs become detached and are ingested by fish and shellfish.
common.name: Worn toothbrush

badly damaged: 🔴 A beige plastic cylinder completely shredded at the edges, showing deep holes.
🔴 This synthetic cap has been eaten away by drilling marine organisms until the bone.
🔴 A cap discolored by the sun, become brittle and crumbling into small pieces of plastic.
damaged: 🟠 A synthetic cork split along its length by pressure.
🟠 This beige plastic shows corkscrew scratches and dried slime deposits.
🟠 A synthetic cap deformed by humidity and covered with a thin layer of silt.
worn: 🟡 A whole synthetic wine cork, with its winemaker logo still legible on the plastic.
🟡 This pink cap has retained its perfect cylindrical shape despite its marine drift.
🟡 A synthetic cork floating on the surface of the water like a small fishing float.

common.fun_fact: The synthetic cap is made of plastic polymers (coextruded polyethylene). Unlike natural cork, it does not degrade and retains its buoyancy for centuries.
common.name: Synthetic cork cap

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of dried rubber, cracked and brittle at the slightest stretch.
🔴 This brown rubber band has broken and has lost all elasticity, covered in black mud.
🔴 A ring of latex shredded into several small pieces stuck to a sargassum.
damaged: 🟠 A slack rubber band, with micro-cracks on its brown surface.
🟠 This rubber band shows pronounced areas of wear caused by sea salt.
🟠 A rubber band tangled with pieces of nylon and strands of seagrass.
worn: 🟡 A classic brown office rubber band, damp but having retained its flexibility.
🟡 This rubber shows a slightly sticky texture due to sun exposure.
🟡 An elastic ring floating between two waters in the calm current of the canal.

common.fun_fact: Natural rubber (latex) elastic degrades more quickly than synthetic polymers, but it remains dangerous because seabirds often confuse it with fleshy marine worms.
common.name: Office rubber band

badly damaged: 🔴 A tiny wire twisted into a U, completely eaten away by rust and brittle.
🔴 A metal staple covered with a matrix of crumbly orange iron oxide.
🔴 A rusty staple debris encrusted in a piece of damp cardboard in decomposition.

🟠 This small piece of metal shows signs of gray oxidation characteristic of steel.
🟠 A deformed staple whose two tips are no longer parallel.
worn: 🟡 A clean metal staple, having retained its original rectangular shape.
🟡 This small shiny wire shows slight traces of humidity without major oxidation.
🟡 A desk staple stuck on a piece of floating paper.

common.fun_fact: The metal clip made of galvanized steel or copper undergoes accelerated corrosion in a marine environment. Its sharp points can puncture the digestive system of animals if they accidentally swallow it.
common.name: Metal clip

badly damaged: 🔴 A completely rusted and deformed capsule, whose logo has become illegible.
🔴 This notched metal disc crumbles into orange dust under finger pressure.
🔴 A crushed capsule and oxidized encrusted in an amalgam of gravel and silt.

🟠 The interior plastic seal has partially detached from the oxidized metal crown.
🟠 A twisted beer cap with notched teeth bent inward.
worn: 🟡 A clean bottle cap, still showing the design of the drink brand.
🟡 This capsule has retained its small regular notches without traces of deformation.
🟡 A metallic soda capsule glowing in the wet sand near the water.

common.fun_fact: The steel beer cap (crown) takes between fifty and one hundred years to degrade. Its inner plastic seal separates from the metal and contributes to microscopic plastic pollution.
common.name: Beer capsule

badly damaged: 🔴 A sheet of aluminum torn into tiny silver shreds stuck to mud.
🔴 This piece of thin metal is bent and crumpled to the point of looking like organic waste.
🔴 A cover whose plastic film has come off and floats separately.

🟠 An aluminized lid with rigid folds filled with wet slime.
worn: 🟡 An entire aluminum yogurt pot lid, crumpled but legible.
🟡 This soft lid shows dried glue residue around its edge.
🟡 A sheet of thin aluminum gleaming beneath water near the shore.

common.fun_fact: The yoghurt lid is often made of covered aluminum foil of plastic film. Very light, it flies away easily and ends up in waterways in the form of shiny debris.
common.name: Yoghurt lid

badly damaged: 🔴 A split red plastic lighter, without a metal wheel or sparking stone.
🔴 This blue lighter has lost its metal head and its tank is filled with black slime.
🔴 A piece of lighter plastic melted and distorted by high heat.
damaged: 🟠 A scratched disposable lighter with its metal dial rusted and blocked by sand.

🟠 A yellow lighter with a broken gas valve full of marine debris.
worn: 🟡 An entire disposable blue plastic lighter, with its metal parts intact.
🟡 This lighter shows slight scratches from use before being lost on the edge of the lake.
🟡 An ordinary lighter floating flat on the water thanks to the air in its tank.

common.fun_fact: The disposable lighter contains steel parts, a spring and a robust plastic reservoir (nylon). It does not degrade and the residual gas can contaminate the water if it leaks.
common.name: Empty disposable lighter

badly damaged: 🔴 A fragment of white glass broken in two, revealing a new dangerous sharp edge.
🔴 This piece of frosted glass has internal cracks ready to shatter it.
🔴 A scrap of frosted glass covered with a black deposit of fuel oil or heavy tar.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of white frosted glass with circular impacts on its matte surface.
🟠 This fragment shows irregular edges that have not finished being rounded by the sand.
🟠 A frosted glass with limescale stains that mask its translucency.
worn: 🟡 A piece of white frosted glass with a perfect matte appearance, soft as candy.
🟡 This fragment has a translucent oval pebble shape, perfectly polished by the ocean.
🟡 A beautiful piece of white frosted glass glowing gently under clear water.

common.fun_fact: Frosted glass (sea glass) is ordinary glass polished by the continuous movement of waves on the sand. This process takes between twenty and one hundred years to give the fragment a smooth, matte texture.
common.name: Piece of frosted glass

badly damaged: 🔴 A ball of aluminum crushed and encrusted with black mud, impossible to unfold.
🔴 This aluminum foil is crumbling into small, very shiny silver confetti.
🔴 A piece of crumpled aluminum covered with a greasy gray kitchen film.
damaged: 🟠 A sheet of aluminum foil crumpled into a loose ball, containing fine sand.
🟠 The thin metal shows tears on its angular folds and discoloration.
🟠 A deformed silver pellet with stuck-on pieces of paper packaging.
worn: 🟡 A crumpled ball of aluminum foil clean, shiny in the sunlight.
🟡 This piece of aluminum has retained its silvery shine despite its aquatic stay.
🟡 A crumpled sheet of aluminum foil floating gently like a silver sheen.

common.fun_fact: Crumpled aluminum foil takes several decades to oxidize. Its shine attracts the curiosity of predatory fish who try to swallow it, causing serious internal injuries.
common.name: Crumpled aluminum foil

badly damaged: 🔴 A tiny pink plastic disk discolored and split into microscopic pieces.
🔴 This green confetti is stuck to a grain of sand by a stagnant black mud.
🔴 Plastic confetti deformed by heat, resembling small dirty grains.
damaged: 🟠 A blue plastic confetti showing scratches and loss of shine.
🟠 This small plastic circle shows jagged edges caused by biting of organisms.
🟠 A crumpled yellow confetti stuck to a tree leaf decaying.
worn: 🟡 A round, clean red plastic confetti, shiny under the water of the port.
🟡 This confetti has retained its perfect circular shape and original bright color.
🟡 A tiny sliver of shiny plastic floating between two waters in the current.

common.fun_fact: The plastic confetti used at outdoor parties is carried away by wind and rain. Due to their microscopic size, they are impossible to collect and pollute instantly.
common.name: Plastic Confetti

badly damaged: 🔴 A white latex glove torn into sticky shreds, filled with stinking black slime.
🔴 This glove has lost all its fingers and its rubber shows deep cracks.
🔴 A decomposed, slimy latex membrane wrapped around a tree root.

🟠 The elastic material has lost its original flexibility under the effect of salt and sun.
🟠 A disposable glove with holes filled with coarse sand and small marine gravel.
worn: 🟡 A crumpled but whole white latex glove, floating like a ghostly hand.
🟡 This glove shows slight dirt from use without deformation of its structure.
🟡 A disposable glove floating on the surface of the calm water like a deflated balloon.

common.fun_fact: The latex glove degrades faster than nitrile gloves, but it takes several years to disappear. It presents a threat of suffocation to marine mammals that swallow it.
common.name: Disposable latex glove

badly damaged: 🔴 A shredded blue mask whose horizontal folds are full of active black slime.
🔴 This surgical mask has lost its metal nose clip and its two side elastics.
🔴 A frayed blue plastic cloth, wrapped tightly around wooden debris.
damaged: 🟠 A blue surgical mask torn in the middle, with a side elastic hanging down.
🟠 The metal nose bar shows traces of red rust which stain the blue fabric.
🟠 A crumpled mask filled with sand and broken shells on the shore.
worn: 🟡 A whole blue surgical mask, with its two elastics intact and its clip in place.
🟡 This mask shows superficial soiling after floating for a few days.
🟡 A disposable mask floating on the surface of the water like a blue synthetic jellyfish.

common.fun_fact: Disposable surgical masks are made of polypropylene. They take more than four hundred and fifty years to degrade, and their elastics cause the strangulation of many animals.
common.name: Mask surgical

badly damaged: 🔴 A wooden stick split in two, blackened by slime and crumbling under the fingers.
🔴 This piece of flat wood shows signs of advanced decomposition by maritime bacteria.
🔴 A piece of broken wooden stick with loose, spongy fibers.
damaged: 🟠 A wooden ice cream stick with chips on its rounded edges.
🟠 The surface wood shows gray moisture stains and crab bite marks.
🟠 A wooden stick deformed by water, with remains of brand inscription deleted.
worn: 🟡 An entire wooden ice cream stick, clean but damp and permeated with river smell.
🟡 This stick has retained its perfect spatula shape despite its aquatic stay.
🟡 A small light wooden stick floating on the surface near the sandy bank.

common.fun_fact: The beech or birch wood ice stick is biodegradable and takes a few months to disappear. It is the least polluting waste on this list, although it reflects the abandonment of rubbish.
common.name: Wooden ice cream stick

badly damaged: 🔴 A price tag reduced to an empty transparent plastic film, the paper having disappeared.
🔴 This piece of thin plastic is torn into tiny shreds stuck to mud.
🔴 A piece of blackened plastic label stuck to decomposing algae.

🟠 A distorted price tag with traces of sticky glue on the reverse.
worn: 🟡 An entire laminated price tag, still showing printed price figures.
🟡 This tag has retained its perfect rectangular shape despite its marine drift.
🟡 A small plastic tag floating between two waters in the calm current.

common.fun_fact: The laminated price tag combines paper and a synthetic protective film. It does not biodegrade and the printed text eventually fades under the abrasive action of water.
common.name: Price tag plasticized

badly damaged: 🔴 A coiled iron wire completely eaten away by rust and breaking at the slightest stretch.
🔴 This metal spiral crumbles into brittle pieces of orange iron oxide.
🔴 A rusty iron wire embedded in a porous block of marine limestone.

🟠 A twisted metal wire showing residues of worn plastic sheath.

🟡 This metal shows light signs of moisture without major oxidation.
🟡 A metallic iron twist shining in the wet sand near the water.

common.fun_fact: Twisted iron wire, although oxidizable, is made of steel or iron. It can be sorted in the yellow bin because ferrous metals are easily captured by magnets in sorting centers to be recycled.
common.name: Twisted iron wire

badly damaged: 🔴 A fragment of very black rubber frayed, releasing its rusty interior steel wires.
🔴 This piece of tire shows signs of advanced degradation from prolonged friction.
🔴 A piece of heavy, stinking rubber embedded in compact black slime.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of black tire with clear tears and deep scratches.

🟠 A deformed tire fragment containing coarse sand embedded in the grooves.
worn: 🟡 A piece of sturdy black rubber tire, still showing the tread pattern.
🟡 This tire debris has its edges rounded by the continuous movement of the waves.
🟡 A fragment of heavy rubber lying at the bottom of the clear water near the shore.

common.fun_fact: Shredded tire pieces release carbon black, heavy metals (zinc, lead) and toxic organic compounds. They are a major source of pollution of aquatic sediments.
common.name: Piece of shredded tire

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of treated wood split into several splinters black and spongy, eaten away by shipworms.
🔴 This board debris shows advanced decomposition with a chemical smell.
🔴 A fragment of brittle green treated wood, covered in a black greasy mud.

🟠 The characteristic green surface shows gray moisture stains and scratches.
🟠 A scrap of treated plank deformed by water, with rusty nails still embedded.
worn: 🟡 A whole piece of autoclave-treated wood, clean but damp and permeated with the smell of softwood.
🟡 This green wood debris has retained its rectangular cut shape despite its drift.
🟡 A scrap of green treated wood floating horizontally near the sandy bank.

common.fun_fact: Autoclaved treated wood contains toxic fungicidal and insecticide agents (such as copper or chromium). Its immersion releases these chemical substances which poison micro-organisms.
common.name: Treated wood debris

badly damaged: 🔴 A completely frayed piece of green nylon cord, resembling a wig of algae.
🔴 This blue cord has lost its original braided structure under the action of the surf.
🔴 A synthetic cable blackened and hardened by the sun, covered in stinking silt.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of white nylon cord with broken links and knots.
🟠 This rope shows traces of wear by prolonged friction against rocks at the bottom.
🟠 A coiled cord still containing wood debris and small shells.
worn: 🟡 A piece of clean blue nylon cord, the braid of which is still strong.
🟡 This synthetic rope floats gently between two waters without obstacle.
🟡 A yellow nylon cord washed up on the beach by high tide.

common.fun_fact: Nylon cord is extremely resistant to traction and abrasion. Lost at sea, it slowly unravels into small filaments that trap crabs and birds.
common.name: Nylon cord

badly damaged: 🔴 A metal nail completely eaten away by rust, whose head breaks under the fingers.
🔴 This steel rod is crumbling into crumbly orange iron oxide pieces.
🔴 A rusty nail embedded in a block of decomposing driftwood.
damaged: 🟠 A twisted steel nail with traces of brown oxidation all over its surface.
🟠 This nail shows deep points of corrosion which weaken its metallic structure.
🟠 A deformed nail showing residue of mortar or dried paint.

🟡 This nail shows a thin film of brown rust due to a short stay underwater.
🟡 An ordinary framing nail found stuck in seaweed.

common.fun_fact: The ordinary steel nail degrades, releasing iron oxide (rust), a natural, non-toxic chemical compound. However, its pointed shape presents an immediate physical danger.
common.name: Rusty nail

badly damaged: 🔴 A steel rivet completely covered in a thick orange iron oxide matrix.
🔴 This metal piece crumbles into crumbly rust dust under pressure.
🔴 An oxidized rivet embedded in a piece of decomposing sheet metal background.
damaged: 🟠 A steel rivet with traces of surface rust and major deformation.
🟠 The round head of the rivet shows deep corrosion spots on its edges.
🟠 A deformed rivet showing residue of flaking gray anti-rust paint.
worn: 🟡 A clean steel rivet, retaining its original cylindrical shape.
🟡 This metal rivet shows light traces of moisture with no major oxidation.
🟡 An ordinary assembly rivet found in wet sand near water.

common.fun_fact: The steel rivet is used in shipbuilding and industrial construction. Its high iron content makes it susceptible to rust, but its massive structure takes decades to disappear from the bottom.
common.name: Rivet steel

badly damaged: 🔴 A fragment of white tile broken into very sharp, pointed pieces.
🔴 This piece of ceramic has edges ready to slice through a fishing line.
🔴 Micro-debris of glazed tiles mixed with coarse sand in the mud.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of broken tiles with traces of adhesive mortar on the back.
🟠 The blue enamel of this tile shows significant scratches and chips on its edges.
🟠 A fragment of earthenware with stains of limestone that obscures its pattern.
worn: 🟡 A piece of wave-polished tile, with edges soft and opaque in appearance.
🟡 This ceramic debris has lost its sharpness after years of back and forth in the sand.
🟡 A fragment of rounded glazed tile, soft to the touch like a pebble.

common.fun_fact: Ceramic tiles are made from clay fired at high temperatures. It is an inert waste that does not chemically pollute water, but its broken pieces remain sharp.
common.name: Piece of broken tiles

badly damaged: 🔴 A torn tea bag whose decomposing tea leaves form a black mud.
🔴 This sachet has lost its thread and its paper label, reduced to a dirty membrane.
🔴 A sachet tea in synthetic shreds wrapped around seaweed.
damaged: 🟠 A tea bag swollen with water with a tear letting its contents escape.
🟠 The paper of the sachet shows brown stains and a loss of its original strength.
🟠 A used tea bag with bite marks from small fish.
worn: 🟡 A whole tea bag, with its damp string and cardboard label.
🟡 This bag shows slight surface dirt after floating for a few days.
🟡 A disposable tea bag floating on the surface of the water calm like a leaf.

common.fun_fact: Modern tea bags often contain plastic fibers (polypropylene) to seal the bag. They do not degrade entirely and release millions of microplastics.
common.name: Tea bag

badly damaged: 🔴 A shredded red plastic candy bar wrapper, covered in black silt.
🔴 This packaging has lost its shine and artwork in the sun.
🔴 A remnant of plastic packaging stuck to a piece of decomposing sargassum.

🟠 The shiny plastic shows wrinkles discolored from prolonged exposure to water.
🟠 A crumpled wrapper filled with fine sand and small algae green.
worn: 🟡 An entire candy bar wrapper, crumpled but legible, washed up on the beach.
🟡 This plastic packaging shows traces of moisture inside the bag.
🟡 A plastic package floating on the surface of the water like a colorful jellyfish.

common.fun_fact: The candy bar wrapper is made of shiny polypropylene. Very resistant to humidity, it drifts in waterways without changing, visually polluting rivers.
common.name: Candy bar wrapper

badly damaged: 🔴 A white plastic lid broken in two, with major dirty cracks.
🔴 This piece of plastic no longer has its opening and its flange is torn off.

🟠 The white wall shows significant scratches and deposits of mud inside.
🟠 A white lid, scratched on all sides, filled with sand and empty shells.
worn: 🟡 A classic white plastic cover whole, wet but still retaining its shape.
🟡 This cover shows slight traces of use before it was abandoned in the channel.
🟡 A white plastic cover floating on the surface of the calm water of the harbor.

common.fun_fact: The takeaway coffee lid is made of white polystyrene. Very fragile, it quickly cracks under the action of UV rays, contributing significantly to plastic pollution in urban waterways.
common.name: Takeaway coffee lid

badly damaged: 🔴 A crushed plastic toothpaste tube, split into pieces and without its cap.
🔴 This piece of plastic no longer has its upper flange and its bottom is torn off.
🔴 A tube of toothpaste crumpled and deformed by the passage of a boat propeller.
damaged: 🟠 An empty tube of toothpaste with cracks on its wrinkled sides.
🟠 Soft plastic shows heavy bending and silt deposits.
🟠 A tube of toothpaste discolored by the sun, filled with wet sand.

🟡 This tube shows light signs of use before being carried away by the current.
🟡 A tube of plastic toothpaste floating horizontally near the rocks.

common.fun_fact: The toothpaste tube contains several layers of plastic and sometimes a thin sheet of aluminum. This multilayer structure makes it a complex waste that is impossible to recycle in a conventional manner.
common.name: Empty toothpaste tube

badly damaged: 🔴 A button cell battery completely covered in a crust of corrosive white and green oxidation.
🔴 This small metal disk is crumbling and showing internal chemical leaks.
🔴 A rusty button battery embedded in an amalgam of black sand and silt.
damaged: 🟠 A flat button battery with traces of red rust on its edges round.
🟠 The metal envelope shows deep points of corrosion which threaten its structure.
🟠 A small button battery deformed by an impact, with deposits of sea salt.
worn: 🟡 A clean metal button battery, retaining its original silver shine.
🟡 This small disc shows slight traces of humidity without major oxidation.
🟡 A used button battery found in the wet sand near the edge of the lake.

common.fun_fact: The button cell contains lithium, zinc or mercury. In a humid environment, its metal shell oxidizes quickly, releasing these heavy metals that are highly toxic to river fish.
common.name: Used button battery

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of twisted wire mesh completely eaten away by rust and brittle.
🔴 These metal meshes crumble into crumbly rust dust under pressure.
🔴 A fragment of rusty mesh embedded in a broken dike concrete block.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of warped wire mesh with brown rust marks on the joints.
🟠 The mesh structure shows significant twisting caused by debris.
🟠 A metal mesh with residue of torn green plastic sheath.
worn: 🟡 A clean piece of chicken wire, having retained its regular mesh shape.
🟡 This mesh shows slight signs of moisture without major oxidation.
🟡 A fragment of metal mesh lying at the bottom of the clear water near the edge.

common.fun_fact: The piece of galvanized or plastic-coated steel mesh takes decades to rust. Its twisted mesh creates a static net that can injure or trap small aquatic animals.
common.name: Piece of mesh

badly damaged: 🔴 A gray and spongy cellulose pulp that disintegrates at the slightest contact in the current.
🔴 A completely flattened paper cup residue, covered with black silt and slimy mold.
🔴 Torn and black cardboard shreds where the red advertising logo is almost entirely washed away.
damaged: 🟠 A paper cup softened and deformed by moisture, split along its entire length.
🟠 This soda cup shows a loss of watertightness, with its plastic lid torn and filled with silt.
🟠 A cardboard container whose neck is crushed and stained by clay deposits.
worn: 🟡 A paper cup still whole but waterlogged, floating vertically near the riverbank.
🟡 This disposable fast-food cup has kept its cylindrical shape despite some surface creases.
🟡 A discarded paper cup lost recently, its outer branding label still legible.

common.fun_fact: The branded paper cup seems eco-friendly at first glance, but it hides a concerning reality. To make it leak-proof for water and hot drinks, manufacturers line it with a thin inner layer of polyethylene plastic. When it ends up in a river or the sea, the paper fibers decompose in a few weeks, but this plastic film persists for decades. As it breaks down under the mechanical action of waves and salt, it fragments into thousands of invisible microplastic particles that directly enter the bodies of fish and shellfish, seriously polluting the food chain.
common.name: Branded paper cup

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of plastic film yellowed by UV rays, which has become brittle and covered with small attached mollusks.
🔴 Shreds of clear acetate frayed into tangled filaments caught in rotting twigs.
🔴 A rigid and cracked plastic sheet, half-buried under the sand and gravel of the riverbed.
damaged: 🟠 A clear plastic sleeve creased and stretched, containing cloudy and sandy water.
🟠 This document protector shows deep opaque scratches and tears at its welded seam.
🟠 A flexible plastic sleeve deformed by the current, covered with a thin lime deposit.
worn: 🟡 A clear plastic sleeve almost intact, slippery and covered with a thin veil of silt.
🟡 This lightweight document protector floats just below the surface of the water like a synthetic jellyfish.
🟡 A flexible plastic sleeve discarded recently, still smooth despite some creases.

common.fun_fact: The clear plastic sleeve, commonly used to archive or protect administrative documents, is made of polypropylene or lightweight PVC. Its prolonged stay in sea or river water exposes it to solar ultraviolet rays that degrade its polymers. It then becomes brittle and fragments into smaller and smaller pieces. These translucent plastic microparticles mimic plankton or small marine prey, deceiving the vigilance of fish and birds that ingest them, causing physical blockages. Marine currents transport them over thousands of kilometers, creating zones of floating pollution.
common.name: Transparent plastic bag

badly damaged: 🔴 A thin slip of grayish paper, which has become slimy and tears into tiny fibers underwater.
🔴 A shapeless mush of cellulose pulp blackened by thermal marine oxidation.
🔴 A shriveled piece of chemical paper, mixed with deposits of foul silt.
damaged: 🟠 A cash receipt crumpled into a tight ball, holding algae debris and black sand.
🟠 This store receipt shows a washed-out surface where the thermal ink has completely disappeared under water.
🟠 A split and fragile thermal paper, whose fibers are slowly detaching in the current.
worn: 🟡 A cash receipt made of smooth paper still legible, recently discarded on the wet sand of the riverbank.
🟡 This commercial transaction receipt is slightly creased and faded on its outer edges.
🟡 A used cash receipt floating on the surface of fresh water, still whole and identifiable.

common.fun_fact: The thermal cash receipt poses a major ecological problem due to its chemical coating. Unlike standard paper, thermal paper contains color developers such as Bisphenol A (BPA) or Bisphenol S (BPS). When these receipts are discarded in nature and come into contact with water, these endocrine disruptors dissolve quickly and contaminate the aquatic environment. They alter the reproductive system of amphibians and fish, even at tiny concentrations. Thermal inks also contain chemical solvents that alter the microbiological quality of the water.
common.name: Thermal receipt receipt

badly damaged: 🔴 A polypropylene film crumpled to the extreme, trapped in a decaying clump of filamentous algae.
🔴 A thin layer of flexible plastic encrusted with a film of dark grease and silt.
🔴 Tiny fragments of shiny plastic floating on the surface like microscopic confetti.
damaged: 🟠 A torn clear protective film from a cigarette pack, filled with fine sandy silt.
🟠 This flexible plastic shows significant elongation due to the mechanical deformation of the waves.
🟠 A translucent plastic film cracked on its folds, holding organic debris.
worn: 🟡 A protective plastic film almost intact, floating discretely between two water layers.
🟡 This packaging plastic retains its rectangular geometry despite light crumpling.
🟡 A thin plastic film recently washed onto the silt of the bank by the river's backwash.

common.fun_fact: The cigarette pack plastic film is made of oriented polypropylene, an ultra-resistant and very light plastic polymer. Because of its low density, this waste floats very easily on the surface or between two water layers, traveling long distances in rivers to the ocean. Under the action of surf and salt, it fragments into microparticles that absorb other persistent organic pollutants present in the water. This type of lightweight plastic waste is often blown by the wind from beaches directly into the sea.
common.name: Cigarette pack wrap

badly damaged: 🔴 A small, gathered piece of aluminum and plastic debris, its colors faded by salt.
🔴 A foil wrapper crumpled into a compact ball and embedded in clayey silt.
🔴 Torn and shiny metallic micro-debris, wedged between two pebbles at the bottom of the water.
damaged: 🟠 A chewing gum wrapper folded showing slight tears and deposits of fine sediment.
🟠 This small packaging bag shows abrasive scratches that have erased the commercial brand.
🟠 A foil wrapper folded on itself, containing sand and micro-algae.
worn: 🟡 A shiny silver metallic chewing gum wrapper still bright, floating adrift.
🟡 This small flexible wrapper has kept its colorful patterns visible despite a short stay underwater.
🟡 A chewing gum paper discarded recently, barely crumpled and resting on a wet stone.

common.fun_fact: The chewing gum wrapper is often composed of a multi-layer complex combining paper, aluminum, and a protective plastic film. This hybrid structure makes its recycling particularly difficult and its decomposition extremely slow. In water, the paper dissolves but the aluminum and plastic film persists for more than a hundred years. Its metallic luster attracts many seabirds that mistake it for small shiny fish and poison themselves by swallowing it. The combined metals and plastics also block the passage of light necessary for aquatic plants.
common.name: Chewing gum wrapper

badly damaged: 🔴 A rigid plastic cap faded by the sun, its thread eroded and covered with brown algae.
🔴 An HDPE cap split in two, showing clear signs of fish bites.
🔴 A plastic ring and its cap encrusted with lime and hard sandy sediment.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic cap showing deformations due to crushing under pebbles at the bottom.
🟠 This bottle cap has lost its original color and shows deep, rough scratches.
🟠 A hard plastic cap half-filled with gray clayey silt and organic residues.
worn: 🟡 A brightly colored plastic cap floating on the water surface like a small buoy.
🟡 This screw cap has kept its internal seal despite some signs of wear.
🟡 A bottle cap discarded recently, still clean and deposited on a bed of pebbles.

common.fun_fact: The plastic bottle cap, usually made of high-density polyethylene (HDPE) or polypropylene (PP), is among the waste items most frequently found on beaches worldwide. Its small size and high buoyancy allow it to drift very far. At sea, seabirds, especially albatrosses, ingest these colored caps, mistaking them for food, which causes fatal intestinal blockages. Systematic sorting and recycling of these small caps make it possible to manufacture new useful objects.
common.name: Plastic bottle capsule

badly damaged: 🔴 Frayed blackened nylon mesh knotted around a dead branch, forming a trap for wildlife.
🔴 A heavy synthetic rope decomposing into loose microfibers under the surf.
🔴 A stiff braid of synthetic netting encrusted with muddy silt and leaf debris.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of tennis net showing loose knots and meshes broken in several places.
🟠 This fragment of nylon rope shows severe discoloration and holds many plant debris.
🟠 Synthetic threads twisted and deformed by the pull of river currents.
worn: 🟡 A fragment of tennis net with its mesh still well formed, floating loosely in the water.
🟡 This piece of black synthetic net has kept its rigid texture despite some surface scratches.
🟡 A piece of used sports net discarded recently, resting near the sandy bank.

common.fun_fact: The piece of tennis net is made of high-strength braided nylon or polyethylene fibers, designed to withstand weather and impacts. Once in the aquatic environment, this net becomes extremely dangerous due to the phenomenon of 'ghost fishing.' Its robust mesh continuously traps fish, birds, and small aquatic mammals that get entangled in it and eventually die. Synthetic mesh can persist for centuries, constantly trapping new marine organisms.
common.name: Piece of tennis net

badly damaged: 🔴 A metal lid twisted and perforated by rust, its inner rubber seal crumbling away.
🔴 A metal washer encrusted with red oxidation and hard calcareous sediment at the bottom of the water.
🔴 A tinplate disc perforated by marine corrosion, leaving only a sharp ring.
damaged: 🟠 A metal lid showing large brown rust spots on its screen-printed decorations.
🟠 This jar lid shows structural deformations and sharp oxidized edges.
🟠 A metal lid whose protective coating is peeling off, releasing paint residues.
worn: 🟡 A metal jam jar lid clean but showing superficial scratches.
🟡 This metal lid has kept its circular screw threads intact despite a short stay in the water.
🟡 A tinplate lid discarded recently, still shining underwater on a bed of light pebbles.

common.fun_fact: The jam jar lid is a metal lid made of tinplate (steel coated with a thin layer of tin) equipped with a plastic seal (plastisol) inside to ensure a tight fit. Although steel is infinitely recyclable, its discharge into water leads to rapid corrosion that releases iron oxides into the ecosystem. In addition, the sharp edges of the rusted metal pose a direct danger of injury to aquatic wildlife. Rusted metallic debris also alters the local acidity of the water at the bottom of the river.
common.name: Jam jar lid

badly damaged: 🔴 A cracked and yellowed thermoformed plastic shell, half-buried in foul silt.
🔴 A boiled, torn cardboard backing still stuck to fragments of clear plastic film.
🔴 Rigid plastic packaging fragments that have become opaque and brittle under the sun.
damaged: 🟠 A toothbrush packaging folded and half-open, trapping fine silt and algae debris.
🟠 This blister pack shows cracked clear plastic and a backing card softened by water.
🟠 A thermoformed package deformed by mechanical crushing, holding sand and gravel.
worn: 🟡 A toothbrush packaging empty and whole, floating on the water surface like a bubble.
🟡 This rigid plastic blister has kept its thermoformed shape despite minor scratches.
🟡 A plastic and cardboard package discarded recently, with both parts still sealed together.

common.fun_fact: The toothbrush packaging is a dual-material blister pack consisting of a molded transparent plastic shell (often PET) and a printed cardboard backing. When this waste ends up in water, the cardboard dissolves in a few days, releasing potentially toxic inks, while the plastic shell will take several centuries to degrade. This plastic fragments into harmful microparticles. It is advisable to separate the cardboard from the plastic for optimal sorting. Separating the plastic from the cardboard before sorting remains the best way to limit this waste.
common.name: Toothbrush wrapper

badly damaged: 🔴 A completely tangled and stretched network of green plastic threads, trapping gravel and silt.
🔴 A synthetic mesh bag shredded into thin strips that float like tentacles.
🔴 Plastic net debris hardened by lime and marine micro-organisms.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic grocery net showing torn meshes in several places and stretched by the current.
🟠 This synthetic mesh bag shows pronounced discoloration due to prolonged exposure to UV.
🟠 A tangled synthetic net containing empty shells and residues of aquatic leaves.
worn: 🟡 A plastic grocery net whole but crumpled, floating gently between two water levels.
🟡 This green mesh bag has kept its handle and honeycomb structure despite minor surface dirt.
🟡 A used plastic net discarded recently, drifting flat on the surface of the clear water.

common.fun_fact: The plastic grocery net is a lightweight bag made of extruded and welded polyethylene threads in an expandable mesh pattern. This waste represents a high risk of entanglement for aquatic animals. Sea turtles, fish, and birds can get their flippers, beaks, or necks trapped in it, which limits their movements and condemns them to starvation or suffocation. Fish trapped in these stretchable meshes attract other predators which in turn become entangled.
common.name: Plastic shopping net

badly damaged: 🔴 A thin white plastic cup crushed into sharp shards and mixed with putrid silt.
🔴 Rigid plastic fragments from a split pot, encrusted with a film of black clayey silt.
🔴 A shredded yogurt pot residue with a hole in the bottom, buried under sandy sediment.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic cup deformed by heat, its upper metal lid torn away.
🟠 This white yogurt pot shows wide side cracks and very softened walls.
🟠 A flexible plastic pot crumpled on the sides, containing deposits of sand and fine silt.
worn: 🟡 A whole white plastic yogurt pot, floating vertically on the surface of the water.
🟡 This cylindrical container has kept its conical shape despite some superficial scratches.
🟡 A used yogurt pot discarded recently, clean but without its lid.

common.fun_fact: The empty yogurt pot is a very common plastic waste item made of polystyrene (PS). Although lightweight, this material has high chemical resistance that prevents natural biodegradation. Submerged in a stream or river, it undergoes mechanical wear from waves and currents, eventually breaking into small sharp fragments. These plastic shards are ingested by fish and aquatic birds, causing severe internal lesions and blockages of the digestive tract. The accumulation of these polystyrene pots permanently alters sedimentation in estuarine areas.
common.name: Empty yoghurt pot

badly damaged: 🔴 An opaque plastic bottle completely crushed and split, its cap missing.
🔴 A polyethylene bottle faded by UV rays, encrusted with dried algae and coarse sand.
🔴 Remains of a rigid plastic bottle broken into several sharp pieces trapped in mud.
damaged: 🟠 A shampoo bottle showing major scratches and deformations on its plastic shell.
🟠 This plastic flask shows a paper label peeling off in faded shreds.
🟠 An opaque bottle deformed by impacts against pebbles, filled with stagnant soapy water.
worn: 🟡 An opaque white plastic shampoo bottle, floating on its side at the surface.
🟡 This flask has kept its elongated shape and flip cap despite some light scratches.
🟡 A used cosmetic bottle discarded recently, still closed and watertight.

common.fun_fact: The empty shampoo bottle is made of high-density polyethylene (HDPE) or polypropylene (PP), polymers designed for mechanical and chemical durability. In rivers, it drifts on the surface and slowly releases the shampoo residues it contains. These surfactants degrade water quality by forming foams that limit the oxygenation of aquatic life. It is therefore crucial to buy refills or use solid shampoos to reduce this plastic waste.
common.name: Empty shampoo bottle

badly damaged: 🔴 A small aluminum tube twisted and pierced, its outer paint peeling off in gray flakes.
🔴 This plastic glue tube is crushed and encrusted with dried clayey sediments.
🔴 Metal fragments shredded and welded to the bottom silt by hard polymerized glue.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic glue tube showing sharp folds and a nozzle blocked by yellowed residues.
🟠 This glue tube shows deep abrasive scratches and a pierced inner metal seal.
🟠 A flexible tube crushed in several places, showing gray dried glue runs.
worn: 🟡 A miniature strong glue aluminum tube, empty but with its red plastic cap.
🟡 This glue tube has kept its original cylindrical shape despite some surface folds.
🟡 A used glue tube discarded recently, floating adrift near the muddy banks.

common.fun_fact: The empty glue tube constitutes a polluting waste item due to the residues of chemical solvents and polymerized adhesives it still contains. Whether made of flexible aluminum or multi-layer plastic, it will take more than a century to physically destroy itself in water. Residual solvents (esters, cyanoacrylates) can slowly dissolve and poison local aquatic microorganisms. Volatile solvents disperse rapidly in the air when this waste is discarded.
common.name: Empty glue tube

badly damaged: 🔴 A polyester cord frayed into a multitude of loose, dirty threads encrusted with black silt.
🔴 This synthetic shoelace has broken down into fiber pieces scattered in submerged roots.
🔴 A shoelace blackened and hardened by calcareous sediments, tightly wrapped around a rotting branch.
damaged: 🟠 A synthetic shoelace showing major snags and tight knots impossible to untie.
🟠 This cord shows pronounced discoloration from the sun and broken plastic tips.
🟠 A polyester shoelace clogged with wet fine sand, having lost all its original flexibility.
worn: 🟡 A red braided polyester shoelace, still strong although wet and dirty.
🟡 This synthetic shoelace has kept its plastic tips intact despite some signs of wear.
🟡 A used shoelace discarded recently, drifting below the surface of the water.

common.fun_fact: The synthetic shoelace is composed of tightly braided polyester or nylon fibers. These synthetic polymers are insensitive to biological decomposition and resist fresh and salt water very well. Over time, mechanical friction against sediments fragments the shoelace into microscopic synthetic microfibers. These fibers persist in the water column and are ingested by filtering mollusks (like mussels and oysters) that poison themselves. Small pieces of polyester also mix with beach sand, altering its texture.
common.name: Synthetic shoelace

badly damaged: 🔴 A polystyrene block crumbled into small white beads scattered in the current.
🔴 A piece of yellowed synthetic foam, brittle and colonized by putrid silt.
🔴 Polystyrene foam debris reduced to a gray, brittle, spongy texture.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of polystyrene broken into several fragments, showing stone impacts.
🟠 This white foam fragment shows abrasive holes and black sand encrustations.
🟠 An expanded polystyrene block deformed by mechanical wave wear and covered with silt.
worn: 🟡 A clean white piece of expanded polystyrene, floating on the surface like foam.
🟡 This plastic foam fragment has kept its original rigidity and honeycomb structure.
🟡 A used polystyrene block floating gently, barely yellowed by sunlight.

common.fun_fact: The piece of expanded polystyrene is composed of 98% air trapped in a styrene polymer structure. Very light and brittle, it breaks easily under currents and wind into millions of small white beads. These beads resemble fish eggs or insect larvae, prompting fish to eat them. Since they are not digestible, they accumulate in their stomachs, leading to death by starvation. This ultra-lightweight synthetic material is very difficult to collect once scattered at sea.
common.name: Piece of expanded polystyrene

badly damaged: 🔴 A highly rusted metal can lid, folded in two and covered with black silt.
🔴 This steel lid crumbles into iron oxide flakes under corrosive marine action.
🔴 A metallic packaging disc covered with rough oxidation pustules and freshwater silt.
damaged: 🟠 A metal can lid showing twisted and very sharp edges.
🟠 This can lid shows large red rust spots and deformation of its surface.
🟠 A metal lid whose easy-open tab has broken off or detached.
worn: 🟡 A shiny metal can lid, clean but slightly scratched.
🟡 This round metal lid keeps its circular grooves intact despite a stay in water.
🟡 A metal can lid discarded recently, resting on a bed of pebbles.

common.fun_fact: The can lid is made of steel coated with a thin layer of tin (tinplate) or aluminum. Abandoned in a river or the ocean, it undergoes active chemical corrosion. Although the iron and tin eventually oxidize completely, forming non-toxic metal oxides, the extremely sharp edges of the lid represent an immediate and fatal risk of injury to fish and divers. The sharp edges of the steel can also damage the fishing gear of professional fishermen.
common.name: Tin can lid

badly damaged: 🔴 A flattened and creased medicine blister pack, its aluminum foil torn to pieces.
🔴 This hard plastic blister pack is brittle and yellowed, encrusted with river clay.
🔴 Shredded blister pack remains, floating between two waters among green algae.
damaged: 🟠 An empty blister pack showing several depressed and pierced plastic cells.
🟠 This mixed package shows an aluminum foil partially peeled and covered with silt.
🟠 A transparent plastic blister pack showing breaks on its rigid edges.
worn: 🟡 A clean, empty medicine blister pack, retaining the shape of its original cells.
🟡 This plastic packaging is creased but retains its shiny back aluminum foil.
🟡 An empty pill blister pack discarded recently, drifting on the surface of the water.

common.fun_fact: The empty medicine blister pack is a composite package consisting of thermoformed plastic (usually PVC) sealed to a thin aluminum film. Due to this intimate combination of two different materials, the blister pack is difficult to recycle and requires specific treatment. Under water, the two layers eventually separate slowly under waves. The aluminum erodes slowly while the PVC persists for centuries. Mixed aluminum and plastic sheets cannot be recycled in a conventional way.
common.name: Empty medication blister

badly damaged: 🔴 A paper straw reduced to a shapeless gray fiber, liquefying at the slightest water movement.
🔴 This decomposing paper tube resembles a soft, faded larva on the muddy bottom.
🔴 Shreds of sticky, yellowed paper fibers, agglomerated with coarse sand.
damaged: 🟠 A paper straw folded and crushed, its cardboard layers peeling off in a spiral.
🟠 This straw shows black mold and has softened due to the effect of water.
🟠 A disposable cardboard tube swollen with water, having lost its rigidity and original color.
worn: 🟡 A paper straw still whole but soft to the touch, floating near the bank.
🟡 This colored paper straw shows slight moisture wrinkles on its cylindrical wall.
🟡 A used cardboard straw discarded recently, drifting vertically in the current.

common.fun_fact: The soggy paper straw was introduced to replace banned single-use plastic straws. Although made of cellulose and therefore biodegradable in a few months in water, its manufacture often requires synthetic glues and colored inks for its decoration. By disintegrating under the effect of water, it releases these chemical compounds into the aquatic environment. In addition, it takes several weeks to disappear and can irritate fish gills. Choosing reusable stainless steel or bamboo straws is an excellent ecological alternative.
common.name: Soggy paper straw

badly damaged: 🔴 A private diary swollen with water whose ink has run, making the entries completely unreadable.
🔴 A stack of yellowed paper pages agglomerated by silt, its cover fraying.
🔴 Manuscript page fragments floating separately in the middle of decaying twigs.
damaged: 🟠 A private diary showing significant folds and a cardboard cover stained with silt.
🟠 This notebook shows pages warped by moisture and a frayed ribbon bookmark.
🟠 A used private diary split on its back edge, holding fine sand between its pages.
worn: 🟡 A private diary closed by a small rusted padlock, barely wet on its cover.
🟡 This personal notebook has kept its rectangular appearance despite superficial scratches.
🟡 A manuscript diary discarded recently, its paper still white and dry inside.

common.fun_fact: This old diary contains handwritten pages made of light paper and a cardboard cover. The cellulose derived from the wood fibers that make it up is fully recyclable. To ensure optimal processing at the mill, its pages must not be soiled by grease or sticky substances, thereby preserving the quality of the regenerated paper pulp.
common.name: Old diary

badly damaged: 🔴 A cardboard package completely crushed and delaminated by water, decomposing into soft fibers.

Although these substances may temporarily inhibit certain microbes, they do not prevent filamentous fungi from slowly breaking down the plant structure and eventually releasing sulfur and carbon.

They integrate into the soil within a few days, providing rapidly available nutrients to stimulate biological activity.
