damaged: 🟠 A damp clump of coffee grounds showing early signs of cottony mold.
🟠 This coffee ground has lost its original aroma and easily crumbles into tiny grains.
🟠 An agglomerated coffee remainder still containing fragments of disintegrated paper.
worn: 🟡 Freshly extracted coffee grounds, still exhaling a pleasant roasted aroma.
🟡 This dark and wet coffee powder is clean and free of any foreign matter.
🟡 A handful of recently discarded ground coffee, retaining its deep brown color.

common.fun_fact: This finely ground organic matter is particularly rich in nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. It actively attracts earthworms, which consume it and incorporate it deep into the soil layers. Additionally, it promotes the development of fungal mycelium and improves the aeration of the decaying earth.
common.name: Coffee grounds

badly damaged: 🔴 A torn-open tea bag with shredded paper letting its black leaves escape.
🔴 This wet tea bag has turned into a spongy, shapeless brownish paste.
🔴 Remains of a tea bag from which the label and string have completely disappeared.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled tea bag stained with tannins, with its cotton thread half broken.
🟠 This paper tea bag shows surface tears revealing the damp tea leaves inside.
🟠 A worn tea bag whose paper has softened and stuck to the surface sediments.
worn: 🟡 A whole and still warm tea bag, discarded after a single infusion.
🟡 This paper tea bag has retained its rectangular shape and its metal staple.
🟡 A clean used tea bag, with its cardboard tag still hanging at the end of the thread.

common.fun_fact: This sachet combines a plant cellulose envelope with leaves rich in tannins and nitrogen. The paper membrane dissolves quickly under the effect of moisture, allowing microorganisms to access the infused tea. This process provides an excellent balance of carbon and nitrogen to regenerate the soil and enrich it.
common.name: Paper tea bag

badly damaged: 🔴 Eggshell fragments reduced to a fine lime dust mixed with muddy silt.
🔴 These egg shards are yellowed and blackened by mud, becoming unrecognizable.
🔴 Fragments of eggshells deeply embedded in decomposing organic matter.
damaged: 🟠 Cracked eggshell pieces, still showing the dried inner membrane.
🟠 These white egg shards present soil stains and slightly dulled edges.
🟠 Partially crushed eggshells, scattered over a bed of damp clay.
worn: 🟡 Clean, white eggshell halves, barely broken during transportation.
🟡 These eggshells have kept their bright whiteness and concave shape.
🟡 A pile of clean, sharp eggshell fragments, discarded after meal preparation.

common.fun_fact: Consisting almost exclusively of calcium carbonate, these shells provide essential minerals to the soil. They do not decompose through direct bacterial action, but dissolve slowly under the effect of organic acids, helping to regulate terrestrial acidity and strengthen plant cell walls over time as they break down.
common.name: Crushed eggshells

badly damaged: 🔴 Walnut shells blackened and split into tiny, almost undetectable pieces.
🔴 These walnut half-shells are covered with a slippery green silt and are crumbling.
🔴 Walnut fragments fossilized by the constant moisture of the clayey soil.
damaged: 🟠 A walnut shell split in two, its interior lined with gray mold.
🟠 This walnut residue shows gnawing marks and a worn, rough surface.
🟠 Empty walnut shell halves that have lost their original light brown color.
worn: 🟡 Very dry, whole walnut shells, recently discarded after being emptied.
🟡 These walnut half-shells have retained their characteristic rough texture and hardness.
🟡 A group of clean walnut shells, showing clean, sharp fracture lines.

common.fun_fact: These highly rigid woody shells are mainly composed of lignin and cellulose. Their dense structure slows down their degradation, which helps to aerate the decaying materials over the long term. They provide a sustainable source of carbon, protecting the soil against excessive compaction and erosion.
common.name: Walnut shells

badly damaged: 🔴 A bread crust turned into a slimy mush of decomposing yeast.
🔴 This bread remainder is covered with a thick coating of blue and green mold.
🔴 A blackened and sticky piece of bread liquefying at the slightest contact with water.
damaged: 🟠 A bread crust softened by ambient moisture, showing small spots of mold.
🟠 This stale bread has lost its hardness and begins to crumble under insect action.
🟠 A piece of baguette showing a spongy, gray crumb from rotting.
worn: 🟡 An extremely hard and dry bread crust, discarded after sitting in the air too long.
🟡 This piece of stale bread is clean and has retained its golden crispy crust.
🟡 A dry bread remnant free of mold, showing a clean knife cut.

common.fun_fact: This bakery residue contains starch, yeast, and nitrogenous proteins. As soon as it is exposed to moisture, it softens very quickly, becoming an ideal target for molds and bacteria. Its rapid decomposition releases soluble carbon nutrients that boost the activity of the terrestrial microbial flora in the ground.
common.name: Stale bread crusts

badly damaged: 🔴 Fallen leaves reduced to stringy, black skeletons in the rich soil.
🔴 This mass of wet leaves is agglomerated into a shapeless vegetable mud.
🔴 Shreds of dried leaves that crumble into black dust at the slightest touch.
damaged: 🟠 Crumpled and holed autumn leaves, stained black by ambient moisture.
🟠 This dead foliage shows clear gnawing marks from terrestrial snails.
🟠 Fallen leaves softened by rain, beginning to lose their foliar structure.
worn: 🟡 Whole, crisp fallen leaves, recently dropped from the tree in autumn.
🟡 This batch of leaves has retained its beautiful original golden and red hues.
🟡 Dry and clean tree leaves, gently accumulated on the dry ground.

common.fun_fact: Rich in carbon, cellulose, and lignin, these leaves form the basis of forest litter. Their slow degradation by fungi produces stable humus that regulates soil moisture. They help to rebalance the carbon-to-nitrogen ratio when mixed with moist green materials to improve the overall quality of the soil.
common.name: Fallen leaves

badly damaged: 🔴 Fermented grass clippings forming a smelly, slimy greenish-yellow block.
🔴 This cut grass is reduced to a foul-smelling, slippery blackish juice.
🔴 Grass blades in advanced decomposition, amalgamated into a clayey mush.
damaged: 🟠 Softened and yellowed grass clippings, emitting a strong silage odor.
🟠 This pile of cut grass shows whitish filaments of early fermentation.
🟠 Wet and compacted grass blades, having lost their original green color.
worn: 🟡 Grass clippings dry on the surface, discarded after the morning mow.
🟡 These green grass blades have kept their freshness despite a slight compaction.
🟡 A pile of freshly cut grass, still fragrant and free of weeds.

common.fun_fact: These cut grass blades are soaked in water and highly soluble nitrogen compounds. Their decomposition is extremely rapid and is accompanied by a rise in temperature due to intense bacterial activity. They provide necessary moisture and accelerate the breakdown of drier carbon-rich materials in the ground.
common.name: Grass clippings

badly damaged: 🔴 Rotten and blackened flower stems, reduced to thin spongy filaments.
🔴 These flower bouquet remains are covered with slimy and gray mold.
🔴 A pile of decomposed flower stalks whose petals have turned to black mud.
damaged: 🟠 Withered and bent flower stems, with yellowed drooping leaves.
🟠 These softened flower stalks show brown rot spots on the node junctions.
🟠 Worn bouquet stems whose corollas detach at the slightest breath.
worn: 🟡 Dry but whole flower stems, discarded after the end of their bloom.
🟡 This faded bouquet has kept its structure although it lost its bright colors.
🟡 Recently cut flower stems, showing a beginning of leaf wilting.

common.fun_fact: Composed of flexible cellulose and rich in potassium, these stems decompose easily. They offer an aerated structure that facilitates the passage of oxygen, which is essential for aerobic bacteria. By disintegrating, they return to the soil the trace elements that the plant had drawn for its flowering.
common.name: Faded flower stems

badly damaged: 🔴 Wood twigs completely worm-eaten and brittle, crumbling into sawdust.
🔴 These small twigs are covered with white and spongy wood-decaying fungi.
🔴 Twig debris in advanced decomposition, black and deeply embedded in silt.
damaged: 🟠 Damp wood twigs showing deep cracks along their outer bark.
🟠 These small branch pieces are bent and covered with damp green moss.
🟠 Broken twigs presenting a texture softened by the surrounding soil moisture.
worn: 🟡 Very dry and firm wood twigs, snapping with a clean, sharp sound.
🟡 These thin branches have kept their bark intact despite minor scratches.
🟡 A batch of twigs gathered from the ground, clean and free of sticky soil.

common.fun_fact: These small pieces of branches provide a high proportion of carbon and lignin. Their decomposition requires the slow action of wood-decaying fungi. They play a major physical role by creating air pockets within the organic matter, preventing asphyxiation and rotting of moist elements in the soil structure.
common.name: Wood twigs

badly damaged: 🔴 An egg box completely disintegrated into a grey cellulose mush.
🔴 This paperboard packaging is colonized by black molds and covered with silt.
🔴 Shredded pieces of egg cardboard, soaked in a foul-smelling mud.
damaged: 🟠 A very soft and deformed cardboard egg box, stained with dried egg residue.
🟠 This molded pulp packaging shows major tears along its closure flaps.
🟠 A worn cardboard box retaining fine sand and small twigs in its cups.
worn: 🟡 A whole though damp cardboard egg box, showing light food stains.
🟡 This packaging has retained its molded cellulose rigidity despite minor folds.
🟡 A recently discarded egg box, its gray cardboard just beginning to soften.

common.fun_fact: Made from unbleached wood pulp, this box is a major source of carbon in the form of pure cellulose. The organic stains it contains provide additional nitrogen. It absorbs excess water and offers an ideal medium for the development of mycelial filaments that enrich the surrounding environment over time.
common.name: Soiled cardboard egg box

badly damaged: 🔴 A paper towel reduced to a spongy white paste in shapeless tatters.
🔴 This absorbent paper is completely blackened by silt and tears at the touch.
🔴 Debris of paper towel mixed with mud and decaying leaves.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of paper towel softened by humidity, showing greasy food spots.
🟠 This absorbent sheet presents significant tears and frayed edges.
🟠 A crumpled used paper towel with stuck pieces of organic food debris.
worn: 🟡 A whole though damp paper towel, recently discarded in the organic waste.
🟡 This piece of absorbent paper has retained its embossed patterns despite folds.
🟡 A dry used paper towel, showing only light water stains on its surface.

common.fun_fact: Composed of short-chain cellulose fibers, this paper disintegrates very quickly under the action of water. It serves as carbonaceous food for microorganisms while absorbing excess decomposition juices. It is important that it does not contain any harmful chemicals or synthetic wax residues at all to prevent soil contamination.
common.name: Used paper towel

badly damaged: 🔴 Blackened and shriveled onion skins, clumped into a damp and slimy mass.
🔴 These decomposing onion skins have become translucent and covered with mold.
🔴 Remains of onion in deliquescent shreds, embedded in black potting soil.
damaged: 🟠 Dry but broken onion skins, presenting greyish discolorations on the surface.
🟠 These onion envelopes show moisture stains and torn, ragged edges.
🟠 Withered onion skins having lost their characteristic coppery luster.
worn: 🟡 Very light coppery onion skins, discarded after the preparation of a dish.
🟡 These papery onion skins have retained their crispy and dry texture.
🟡 A set of clean onion skins, shining softly under the ambient light.

common.fun_fact: These dry, colored skins provide carbon and sulfur, a mineral that is highly useful for plant health. Although their smell can be strong, the volatile compounds quickly fade in the air. They are degraded by common bacteria that recycle their fine cellulosic material in just a few weeks to enrich the soil.
common.name: Onion skins

badly damaged: 🔴 An avocado pit split into several black, spongy pieces from advanced rot.
🔴 This pit is covered with an extremely thick layer of white and green mold.
🔴 An avocado seed residue softened by moisture, crumbling under light pressure.
damaged: 🟠 An avocado pit showing a deep crack and a peeling brown outer skin.
🟠 This seed has lost its perfect spherical shape and shows signs of insect bites.
🟠 A damp avocado pit whose outer coat has partially detached from the core.
worn: 🟡 A whole and very smooth avocado pit, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 This large brown seed has retained its original hardness and thin outer skin.
🟡 A clean avocado pit, still wet with the green flesh of the fruit.

common.fun_fact: This very hard pit is composed of dense lignin and starch. It takes several months to decompose, requiring the patient action of soil fungi. It provides a slow-release source of carbon and, by cracking, creates micro-habitats for mites and small springtails of the terrestrial fauna under the soil layers.
common.name: Avocado pit

badly damaged: 🔴 A melon rind reduced to a thin, soft green film covered with dark silt.
🔴 This melon residue is completely liquefied by rot and smells of fermentation.
🔴 A melon skin collapsed on itself, forming a black spongy organic mass.
damaged: 🟠 A withered and softened melon rind, showing patches of orange mold.
🟠 This piece of melon has lost its original color and presents jagged, worn edges.
🟠 A curved melon skin whose internal flesh has partially disintegrated.
worn: 🟡 A still firm melon rind slice, discarded recently after being cut.
🟡 This melon skin has retained its characteristic netted pattern and rigidity.
🟡 A fresh melon rind showing clean knife marks on its remaining pink flesh.

common.fun_fact: This thick rind is full of water and soluble sugars. It liquefies quickly under the action of bacteria, providing the necessary moisture to dry areas of the decaying organic matter. Its balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio allows for complete assimilation without generating any unpleasant odors in the process.
common.name: Melon rind

badly damaged: 🔴 A completely blackened pear core, covered with a thick mat of mold.
🔴 This pear core has collapsed into a slimy and brown organic mass.
🔴 A pear residue in advanced decomposition, half dissolved in standing water.
damaged: 🟠 A withered and brown pear core, showing soft indentations and spots.
🟠 This pear core has lost its stem and presents beetle bite marks.
🟠 A softened pear residue whose flesh is starting to break apart.
worn: 🟡 A recently discarded pear core, its white flesh just beginning to brown.
🟡 This pear core is still firm, with its seeds visible through the flesh.
🟡 A clean pear remainder showing a neat transverse cut from a knife.

common.fun_fact: This pear residue is rich in fructose, water, and pectin. The latter acts as a natural gelling agent that helps maintain moisture in the soil. Microorganisms multiply rapidly around this tender flesh, accelerating the formation of nitrogenous nutrients for neighboring plants to absorb through their roots.
common.name: Pear core

badly damaged: 🔴 Lettuce leaves reduced to a slimy green and translucent mass.
🔴 This lettuce residue is completely liquefied by bacterial putrefaction.
🔴 Sticky green leaf debris, mixed with damp potting soil residues.
damaged: 🟠 Very soft lettuce leaves, browned along the main structural veins.
🟠 This foliage shows tears and patches of white cottony mold.
🟠 Withered lettuce leaves having lost all of their original crispness.
worn: 🟡 Soft but whole lettuce leaves, discarded after being left out of the fridge.
🟡 This lettuce residue has retained its green color despite marked wilting.
🟡 Clean, recently discarded lettuce leaves without any trace of mold.

common.fun_fact: Composed of over ninety percent water, these green leaves provide instant rehydration to dry organic matter. They decompose in just a few days thanks to the action of aerobic bacteria, releasing nitrogen compounds that are easily absorbed by plant roots in the surrounding soil environment. They leave no waste.
common.name: Withered lettuce leaves

badly damaged: 🔴 Blackened and spongy grape stems, breaking apart at the slightest touch.
🔴 This grape skeleton is covered with white and green mold and dark mud.
🔴 Grape stems in advanced decomposition, deeply embedded in silt.
damaged: 🟠 Dry and wrinkled grape stems, showing broken nodes and branchlets.
🟠 These stems have lost their flexibility and present brown rot spots.
🟠 Grape cluster remains softened by the moisture of the ground.
worn: 🟡 Whole and still green grape stems, discarded after consumption.
🟡 These branched stems have retained their characteristic cluster shape.
🟡 A batch of clean grape stems, showing neat cuts at the ends.

common.fun_fact: These small branched structures are rich in cellulose and antioxidant polyphenols. They degrade slowly, offering a porous structure that promotes air circulation within organic materials. Fungal filaments colonize them first to extract stable carbon, which eventually enriches the soil structure for crops.
common.name: Grape stems

badly damaged: 🔴 Peanut shells blackened and crushed, embedded in thick dark silt.
🔴 These seed casings are rotting away and crumble at the slightest touch.
🔴 Peanut shell debris mixed with filaments of black mold.
damaged: 🟠 Peanut shells split and softened by the moisture of the ground.
🟠 These shells show brown soil stains and a loss of their structural stiffness.
🟠 Worn peanut envelopes having lost their original sand-like color.
worn: 🟡 Whole and very dry peanut shells, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 These seed casings have retained their characteristic grid-like texture.
🟡 A group of clean peanut shells, without any trace of mold.

common.fun_fact: These fibrous shells provide an excellent source of carbon with moderate degradation. Their curved shape creates natural air pockets in the soil, preventing compaction. They are slowly digested by soil fungi that break down the cellulose into nutrients for the surrounding vegetation to grow healthily.
common.name: Peanut shells

badly damaged: 🔴 Pistachio shells blackened and split into almost unrecognizable pieces.
🔴 These pistachio casings are covered with a slippery green silt layer.
🔴 Shell fragments deeply embedded in the damp clayey mud.
damaged: 🟠 Pistachio shells showing salt stains and brown soil accumulations.
🟠 These shell pieces have lost their hardness and are starting to crumble.
🟠 Empty pistachio half-shells having lost their original cream color.
worn: 🟡 Very dry, whole pistachio shells, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 These woody shells have retained their boat-like shape and stiffness.
🟡 A pile of clean pistachio shells, showing clean, sharp opening lines.

common.fun_fact: These hard shells contain a high level of lignin, making them resistant to rapid decomposition. They provide carbon over the long term and improve soil drainage. It is best to rinse them beforehand if they are very salty, so as not to disturb the delicate microbial fauna that decomposes them in the ground.
common.name: Pistachio shells

badly damaged: 🔴 Celery stalks reduced to a black, slimy and fibrous organic mush.
🔴 This vegetable remains is in advanced decay, covered with white mold.
🔴 Blackened celery filaments dissolved in wet clayey silt at the bottom.
damaged: 🟠 Withered and softened celery stalks, having lost their characteristic rigidity.
🟠 These celery ribs show brown rot spots along their structural grooves.
🟠 Worn celery remains whose longitudinal fibers are beginning to separate.
worn: 🟡 Celery stalks softened on the surface, discarded after wilting in the air.
🟡 This piece of celery has retained its green color despite a lack of firmness.
🟡 Clean, recently cut celery stems, showing a still fibrous texture.

common.fun_fact: These highly fibrous stalks are full of water, cellulose, and minerals like potassium. They decompose in a few weeks under the action of detritivorous fungi. Their structure helps retain moisture while releasing essential nutrients for the growth of terrestrial microorganisms in the topsoil layers of the ground.
common.name: Softened celery stalks

badly damaged: 🔴 Garlic skins blackened and stuck together by slimy mold filaments.
🔴 These garlic skins are in advanced decay, turning into a whitish mush.
🔴 Fragments of garlic skins mixed with black organic soil residues.
damaged: 🟠 Crumpled garlic skins softened by the surrounding ambient moisture.
🟠 These garlic envelopes show gray spots and a loss of their translucency.
🟠 Withered garlic skins having lost their original pearly appearance.
worn: 🟡 Very light, white garlic skins, discarded after peeling the cloves.
🟡 These dry garlic skins have retained their papery thinness and clove shape.
🟡 A set of clean garlic skins, shining softly under the ambient light.

common.fun_fact: These dry and translucent skins are made of fine cellulose. They provide carbon and contain sulfur compounds with natural repellent properties for certain soil pests. They decompose in just a few weeks, easily incorporating into the surrounding organic matter without leaving any artificial traces behind.
common.name: Garlic skins

badly damaged: 🔴 Tea leaves reduced to a slimy black paste covered with mold filaments.
🔴 This infused tea remainder is dispersed in mud, forming a dark sludge.
🔴 A mass of dry and cracked tea leaves, almost decomposed by the elements.
damaged: 🟠 Damp tea leaves showing the first signs of white cottony mold.
🟠 This infused tea has lost its original aroma and easily crumbles into small debris.
🟠 An agglomerated loose tea remainder containing fine silt in its folds.
worn: 🟡 Recently infused tea leaves, still exhaling a faint herbaceous aroma.
🟡 This mass of wet tea leaves is clean and free of any foreign matter.
🟡 A pile of green tea leaves discarded after a single brewing.

common.fun_fact: These wet leaves are rich in nitrogen and mild organic acids that promote the development of beneficial soil bacteria. They decompose very quickly and improve soil structure. Earthworms particularly appreciate them and accelerate their transformation into assimilable mineral elements for vegetation.
common.name: Infused tea leaves

badly damaged: 🔴 Cabbage leaves reduced to a smelly, slimy green and translucent paste.
🔴 This cabbage remains is liquefied by rot and covered with a slippery film.
🔴 Black cabbage leaf debris, amalgamated with damp potting soil.
damaged: 🟠 Softened and withered cabbage leaves, showing patches of brown rot.
🟠 This cabbage foliage presents significant tears along its thick ribs.
🟠 Withered cabbage leaves having lost their green color and firmness.
worn: 🟡 Whole but softened cabbage leaves, discarded after being sorted.
🟡 This cabbage remainder has retained its crunchy texture on the main veins.
🟡 Clean cabbage leaves discarded recently, showing a slight wilting.

common.fun_fact: Very rich in water, nitrogen, and calcium, these leaves degrade rapidly. They provide an immediate source of energy for soil microorganisms. By liquefying, they also release sulfur compounds which, in small doses, enrich the microbial diversity necessary for terrestrial fertility and plant health as a whole.
common.name: Damaged cabbage leaves

badly damaged: 🔴 A blackened and rotten pineapple rind, covered with a thick white mycelium.
🔴 This pineapple residue has become soft and slimy, collapsing under pressure.
🔴 A pineapple skin in advanced decay, emitting a sharp vinegary smell.
damaged: 🟠 A withered and softened pineapple rind, showing brown rot spots.
🟠 This piece of pineapple skin has lost its characteristic sharp texture on the edges.
🟠 A damp pineapple rind whose fibers are starting to separate from the core.
worn: 🟡 A still firm pineapple rind, discarded recently after cutting the fruit.
🟡 This pineapple skin has retained its yellow and green colors and prickly texture.
🟡 A clean pineapple rind showing neat knife cut marks on the surface.

common.fun_fact: This robust rind is rich in cellulose, lignin, and fruit acids. Although thick, it degrades under the action of fungi and thermophilic bacteria that tolerate its acidity. It provides a good amount of carbon and trace elements that fortify the soil after a few months of organic decomposition in the ground.
common.name: Pineapple rind

badly damaged: 🔴 A peach pit split in two, its internal kernel rotten and blackened.
🔴 This pit is covered with a slippery green mold and is slowly crumbling.
🔴 Blackened peach pit fragments, deeply embedded in damp silt.
damaged: 🟠 A peach pit showing fracture lines and brown soil accumulations.
🟠 This pit has lost its original brown color and presents a worn, rough surface.
🟠 A damp peach seed whose internal kernel has begun to rot.
worn: 🟡 A whole and very dry peach pit, discarded recently after consumption.
🟡 This large brown seed has retained its original hardness and deep grooves.
🟡 A clean peach pit, still carrying the sweet smell of the fruit.

common.fun_fact: This large, rough pit has a very solid, highly lignified structure. It protects a nutrient-rich seed inside. Its pure carbon shell takes over a year to break down under the action of microorganisms, acting as a long-term structure regulator and promoting natural aeration of the surrounding soil layers.
common.name: Peach pit

badly damaged: 🔴 Cherry pits blackened and split, crumbling into small pieces in the soil.
🔴 These small pits are covered with black mold and a slippery silt layer.
🔴 Broken cherry pit debris, deeply embedded in damp clayey mud.
damaged: 🟠 Cherry pits showing surface cracks and brown soil stains.
🟠 These pits have lost their original reddish hue and show signs of wear.
🟠 Softened cherry remains still containing a few whole pits inside.
worn: 🟡 Whole and very dry cherry pits, discarded recently after consumption.
🟡 These small round seeds have retained their original hardness and beige color.
🟡 A pile of clean cherry pits, without any trace of stuck fruit flesh.

common.fun_fact: These small pits are made of dense wood rich in carbon. Due to their small size, they mix easily with other organic materials. They decompose slowly under the action of soil fungi, progressively releasing stable carbon compounds that provide a long-lasting structure to the terrestrial humus in the garden.
common.name: Cherry pits

badly damaged: 🔴 A corn cob completely blackened and spongy, disintegrating under dark silt.
🔴 This corn cob remainder is colonized by white mycelium and easily breaks in two.
🔴 A corn cob in advanced decomposition, reduced to shapeless dark fibers.
damaged: 🟠 A softened and discolored corn cob, showing crushed grain pockets.
🟠 This cob remainder presents gray moisture stains and a loss of rigidity.
🟠 A worn corn cob containing fine sand residues in its structural grooves.
worn: 🟡 A whole and dry corn cob, discarded after being stripped of its kernels.
🟡 This corn cob has retained its characteristic rigid cylinder despite light scratches.
🟡 A clean corn cob remainder, still showing its original light yellow color.

common.fun_fact: This cob support is made of dense cellulose and lignin, forming a rigid cylinder. It absorbs excess moisture while creating essential aeration channels to prevent microbial suffocation. Its decomposition by fungi takes a few months, leaving a rich carbon residue that benefits the earth and crops alike.
common.name: Corn cob

badly damaged: 🔴 Dry tree leaves reduced to powdery black residues beneath the soil layers.
🔴 This mass of dried leaves has become slippery and amalgamated with silt.
🔴 Debris of dried fallen leaves crumbling instantly to the touch.
damaged: 🟠 Crumpled and broken tree leaves, showing gray moisture stains on their surface.
🟠 This dry foliage presents significant tears caused by the wind.
🟠 Dry leaves softened by recent rain, having lost their original crispness.
worn: 🟡 Whole and crisp dry tree leaves, gathered during the autumn season.
🟡 This batch of dead leaves has retained its beautiful original brown hues.
🟡 Dry and clean fallen leaves, gently accumulated on the dry ground.

common.fun_fact: These dried fallen leaves are excellent sources of carbon and dry organic matter. They absorb moisture from wet nitrogenous components and protect the soil from erosion. Their slow decomposition by microorganisms enriches the earth with humic acids, which are essential for long-term plant fertility.
common.name: Dry tree leaves

badly damaged: 🔴 Pizza box cardboard pieces reduced to a foul gray and black cellulosic paste.
🔴 This greasy cardboard is colonized by dark molds and dissolves in the silt.
🔴 Shredded fragments of pizza cardboard, soaked in a smelly, oily mud.
damaged: 🟠 Pieces of pizza cardboard softened by humidity, showing large greasy stains.
🟠 This worn corrugated cardboard presents significant tears and frayed edges.
🟠 A folded pizza cardboard box retaining fine sand and soil in its creases.
worn: 🟡 Pizza box cardboard pieces still rigid despite being stained with oil spots.
🟡 This cardboard has retained its corrugated structure despite minor surface folds.
🟡 A recently discarded pizza box, clean except for a few sauce spots.

common.fun_fact: These cardboard pieces provide pure wood cellulose. Although stained with food grease, the lipids provide energy to thermophilic bacteria and fungi. This material decomposes completely without leaving toxic residues, provided the cardboard is not laminated or printed with synthetic inks or glues of any kind.
common.name: Greasy pizza box cardboard pieces

badly damaged: 🔴 Moldy bread slices reduced to a slimy green paste from advanced rot.
🔴 This rotten bread is entirely covered with a thick coating of gray mold.
🔴 A bread slice in advanced decomposition, breaking apart in standing water.
damaged: 🟠 Bread slices softened by moisture, showing patches of green mold on the crust.
🟠 This moldy bread has lost its firmness and starts to crumble under the fingers.
🟠 A worn bread slice showing a spongy, damp crumb discolored by humidity.
worn: 🟡 Stale and hard bread slices, showing tiny spots of white mold on the edges.
🟡 This bread has retained its sliced shape despite initial drying.
🟡 A recently discarded dry bread remnant, clean and free of major molds.

common.fun_fact: Already colonized by beneficial molds, these bread slices disintegrate almost immediately in a moist environment. They provide organic nitrogen, carbon, and water. Their rapid decomposition stimulates the activity of detritivorous fungi that extend their networks of filaments throughout the soil structure.
common.name: Moldy bread slices

badly damaged: 🔴 Squash seeds blackened and hollowed out, embedded in slimy silt.
🔴 These seeds are rotten and crumble into a dark cellulose mush.
🔴 Decomposed squash seed fragments, mixed with damp potting soil.
damaged: 🟠 Squash seeds split and softened by the moisture of the ground.
🟠 These seeds show gray mold spots and a loss of their outer stiffness.
🟠 Worn seed casings having lost their original white color.
worn: 🟡 Whole and very dry squash seeds, recently discarded after preparation.
🟡 These flat seeds have retained their oval shape and characteristic hardness.
🟡 A group of clean squash seeds, free of any visible mold.

common.fun_fact: These flat seeds are rich in nitrogen, phosphorus, and plant lipids. They provide a dense nutrient supply for soil fauna. Although some may attempt to germinate, the heat generated by bacterial activity quickly neutralizes their germination power, transforming everything into a nutrient-rich humus for soil.
common.name: Squash seeds

badly damaged: 🔴 Crushed tomato remains reduced to a slimy, acidic reddish-black mush.
🔴 This tomato residue is liquefied by decay and covered with white molds.
🔴 Tomato skins and seeds amalgamated into a shapeless paste in the mud.
damaged: 🟠 Softened and withered tomato pieces, showing gray rot spots on the skin.
🟠 These tomato remains present significant tears along the outer skin.
🟠 A crushed tomato having lost its vegetation water and original firmness.
worn: 🟡 Fresh tomato remains discarded recently after meal preparation.
🟡 This piece of tomato has retained its bright red color despite some wrinkles.
🟡 Clean tomato skins showing neat knife cuts on the surface.

common.fun_fact: This acidic and wet residue is rich in water, vitamins, and nitrogen. Its degradation begins in a few hours under the action of yeasts and bacteria. It releases soluble nutrients that hydrate and activate soil microorganisms, thus accelerating the overall cycle of terrestrial organic decomposition in fields.
common.name: Crushed tomato remains

badly damaged: 🔴 Leek greens reduced to a smelly, slimy black and fibrous organic paste.
🔴 This leek residue is in advanced decay, covered with white mold.
🔴 Blackened leek leaf filaments dissolved in wet clayey mud at the bottom.
damaged: 🟠 Withered and softened leek greens, having lost their original rigidity.
🟠 These leek leaves show brown rot spots along their structural grooves.
🟠 Worn leek remains whose fibers are beginning to separate under water.
worn: 🟡 Leek greens softened on the surface, discarded after peeling the vegetable.
🟡 This piece of leek has retained its green color despite a lack of firmness.
🟡 Clean, recently cut leek leaves, showing a still firm texture.

common.fun_fact: These fibrous leaves provide nitrogen, cellulose, and sulfur compounds. They need a few weeks to be assimilated by soil bacteria. As they decompose, they enrich the soil with essential mineral elements and help retain moisture thanks to their interlaced structure, supporting the growth of new plants.
common.name: Withered leek greens

badly damaged: 🔴 A blackened and rotten grapefruit peel, covered with a thick mat of mold.
🔴 This grapefruit residue has become soft and slimy, collapsing under touch.
🔴 A grapefruit skin in advanced decay, emitting a sharp acidic smell.
damaged: 🟠 A withered and softened grapefruit peel, showing brown rot spots on the surface.
🟠 This piece of skin has lost its characteristic texture and presents nibbled edges.
🟠 A damp grapefruit peel whose white inner pith has completely dissolved.
worn: 🟡 A still firm grapefruit peel, discarded recently after squeezing the fruit.
🟡 This grapefruit skin has retained its yellow-pink color and thickness.
🟡 A clean grapefruit peel showing neat knife cut marks on the surface.

common.fun_fact: This bulky peel contains a lot of cellulose and water, as well as essential oils in its zest. Its decomposition takes place over several weeks. It constitutes a good carbon resource and releases valuable micronutrients for soil fungi once the oils have evaporated, supporting the natural ecosystem in the dirt.
common.name: Grapefruit peel

badly damaged: 🔴 A paper coffee filter reduced to a spongy, torn brown paper paste.
🔴 This wet coffee filter has turned into a shapeless residue stuck in the mud.
🔴 Shreds of paper coffee filter in advanced decay, black and extremely fragile.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled coffee filter stained by coffee grounds, showing significant tears.
🟠 This filter paper shows deformations and traces of clayey soil on the surface.
🟠 A worn coffee filter whose paper has softened and stuck to surrounding sediments.
worn: 🟡 A whole though damp paper coffee filter, recently discarded after brewing.
🟡 This filter has retained its conical shape despite minor surface folds.
🟡 A clean used coffee filter, still holding the black grounds inside.

common.fun_fact: This unbleached paper filter provides pure carbon and retains coffee residues rich in nitrogen and potassium. It decomposes very quickly in contact with moisture. Its fibrous consistency is ideal for consumption by earthworms, which transform it into stable organic matter for the enhancement of the soil.
common.name: Used paper coffee filter

badly damaged: 🔴 Paper napkins reduced to a wet white and black cellulosic paste.
🔴 This absorbent paper has liquefied due to decay and tears at the touch.
🔴 Debris of paper napkins mixed with greasy food residues and dark silt.
damaged: 🟠 Paper napkins softened by humidity, showing large food stains on the surface.
🟠 This absorbent paper presents significant tears and frayed, ragged edges.
🟠 Crumpled used paper napkins retaining fragments of damp soil in their folds.
worn: 🟡 Crumpled but whole paper napkins, discarded recently after a meal.
🟡 This paper has retained its embossed patterns despite minor surface folds.
🟡 Dry used paper napkins, showing only light colored food stains.

common.fun_fact: These cellulose wadding napkins disintegrate with the very first rains. They offer an easily accessible carbon supply for the soil bacterial flora. They also absorb excess water from highly humid waste, maintaining an optimal humidity level for the active decomposition work of microorganisms in the ground.
common.name: Soiled paper napkins

badly damaged: 🔴 A cauliflower stalk completely blackened and softened by advanced decay.
🔴 This vegetable residue is covered with a very thick layer of gray mold.
🔴 A cauliflower core in advanced decomposition, breaking apart in wet mud.
damaged: 🟠 A withered and browned cauliflower stalk, showing soft indentations.
🟠 This cauliflower core presents deep cracks and a general loss of firmness.
🟠 A softened cauliflower remainder whose fibers are beginning to separate.
worn: 🟡 A still white and firm cauliflower stalk, recently discarded after cutting.
🟡 This vegetable core has retained its characteristic compact cylinder shape.
🟡 A clean cauliflower remainder showing a neat transverse cut from a knife.

common.fun_fact: This dense stalk is rich in water, cellulosic fibers, calcium, and sulfur. Its degradation requires the combined action of fungi that soften its structure and bacteria that extract its nutrients. It transforms in a few weeks into assimilable organic matter that feeds the surrounding plant root systems.
common.name: Cauliflower stalk

badly damaged: 🔴 Artichoke leaves blackened and softened by rotting processes in the silt.
🔴 These artichoke bracts are disintegrating into dark, fibrous organic pieces.
🔴 Decomposed artichoke leaf fragments, deeply embedded in black muddy silt.
damaged: 🟠 Dry but broken artichoke leaves, showing brown soil stains on the surface.
🟠 These leaves have lost their stiffness and present signs of gray moisture.
🟠 Withered artichoke bracts having lost their original green color.
worn: 🟡 Whole and very dry artichoke leaves, recently discarded after a meal.
🟡 These thick leaves have retained their characteristic pointed tips and hardness.
🟡 A group of clean artichoke leaves, showing clean, sharp fracture lines.

common.fun_fact: These thick leaves contain a high proportion of lignin and silica. They decompose slowly over several months under the action of soil fungi. Their presence helps to aerate the organic matter and provide durable carbon that contributes to the formation of stable humus over time in the terrestrial soil.
common.name: Tough artichoke leaves

badly damaged: 🔴 Wood sawdust reduced to a compact, slimy black cellulosic paste.
🔴 This wood residue is colonized by green molds and dissolves in the mud.
🔴 Sawdust shavings amalgamated into a dark, smelly mush in standing water.
damaged: 🟠 Damp wood sawdust showing discolorations and soft, compacted clumps.
🟠 This pile of sawdust presents traces of brown soil and a loss of fluidity.
🟠 Wet fine shavings having lost their original dry and volatile texture.
worn: 🟡 Very dry, fine wood sawdust, discarded after carpentry work.
🟡 These light wood shavings have retained their pine smell and fluidity.
🟡 A pile of clean wood sawdust, free of dust and foreign matter.

common.fun_fact: This fine sawdust from untreated wood is extremely rich in carbon and very poor in nitrogen. It must be mixed with wet nitrogenous materials to balance. It absorbs excess moisture and serves as a growth medium of choice for wood-decaying fungi that break down lignin, enriching the soil over time and enhancing crop yields.
common.name: Clean wood sawdust

badly damaged: 🔴 Dry straw strands blackened and spongy, crumbling into tiny organic debris.
🔴 These straw stalks are colonized by dark molds and covered with silt.
🔴 Straw debris in advanced decomposition, black and deeply embedded in soil.
damaged: 🟠 Damp straw strands showing bends and gray moisture spots on the surface.
🟠 These stalks have lost their golden luster and present signs of rot.
🟠 Softened straw strands having lost their original stiffness under water.
worn: 🟡 Very golden and firm dry straw strands, recently discarded in the garden.
🟡 These cereal stalks have retained their tubular shape and crisp texture.
🟡 A batch of clean straw strands, free of any traces of sticky soil.

common.fun_fact: These dry cereal stalks provide a massive amount of carbon in the form of cellulose and hemicellulose. They create important air spaces that promote the breathing of aerobic bacteria. Their slow decomposition by fungi helps to structure the soil and retain water, preventing drought damage to crops nearby.
common.name: Dry straw strands

badly damaged: 🔴 A houseplant reduced to rotten black stems and slimy, decaying roots.
🔴 This plant residue is covered with a thick coating of white and gray mold.
🔴 A decomposed green plant forming a shapeless spongy organic mass.
damaged: 🟠 A withered houseplant with yellow, drooping and dry leaves on the stems.
🟠 This plant shows softened stems and rot spots around the root collar.
🟠 Worn green plant remains whose roots are beginning to dry out completely.
worn: 🟡 A dry but whole houseplant, discarded recently after wilting.
🟡 This dead plant has retained its structure of dried stems and leaves.
🟡 A clean houseplant showing dry potting soil residues on its roots.

common.fun_fact: This dead plant combines nitrogen-rich leaves and carbonaceous fibrous stems. Its degradation returns to the soil the mineral salts and trace elements it had stored during its life. Its dry roots provide a porous texture that helps aeration and the development of beneficial soil microorganisms in the ground.
common.name: Withered houseplant

badly damaged: 🔴 Plum pits blackened and split, crumbling into tiny black fragments in the soil.
🔴 These plum pits are covered with black mold and a slippery silt layer.
🔴 Broken plum pit debris embedded in damp clayey mud at the bottom.
damaged: 🟠 Plum pits showing deep cracks and brown soil accumulations on the surface.
🟠 These pits have lost their original brown color and show signs of wear.
🟠 Softened fruit remains still containing a few whole plum pits inside.
worn: 🟡 Whole and very dry plum pits, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 These small brown seeds have retained their characteristic hardness and grooves.
🟡 A batch of clean plum pits, without any trace of stuck fruit flesh.

common.fun_fact: These hard pits have a very dense woody shell that resists moisture. They decompose slowly under the action of microscopic fungi. They enrich the soil with carbon and serve as temporary shelters for small terrestrial invertebrates that participate in the fragmentation of decaying organic matter in the earth.
common.name: Plum pits

badly damaged: 🔴 A shriveled and dried lemon peel, covered with a thick layer of green mold.
🔴 This lemon residue has turned completely black and hardened, almost fossilized.
🔴 Shreds of spongy lemon peel decomposing slowly in the damp mud.
damaged: 🟠 A softened lemon peel showing whitish discolorations on its outer zest.
🟠 This piece of skin has lost its bright yellow color and presents torn edges.
🟠 A withered lemon peel whose white inner pith has completely dissolved.
worn: 🟡 A still fresh and fragrant lemon peel, discarded after being squeezed.
🟡 This lemon skin has kept its beautiful yellow color despite a slight drying.
🟡 A clean lemon peel showing neat knife cut marks on the surface.

common.fun_fact: This yellow peel contains acidic oils and cellulose. Although citrus initially slows down bacteria, its volatile compounds evaporate quickly in the air. It then provides carbon and vitamins useful to soil fungi that finish decomposing it cleanly, enriching the ground with important nutrients over time.
common.name: Lemon peel

badly damaged: 🔴 Hazelnut shells blackened and split into tiny, almost undetectable pieces.
🔴 These hazelnut casings are covered with a slippery green silt and are rotting.
🔴 Hazelnut shell fragments deeply embedded in the damp clayey mud.
damaged: 🟠 Hazelnut shells showing deep cracks and brown soil accumulations on the surface.
🟠 These shell pieces have lost their hardness and start to crumble under water.
🟠 Empty hazelnut half-shells having lost their original light brown color.
worn: 🟡 Very dry, whole hazelnut shells, recently discarded after consumption.
🟡 These woody shells have retained their characteristic rounded shape and stiffness.
🟡 A pile of clean hazelnut shells, showing clean, sharp fracture lines.

common.fun_fact: These small, very hard wooden casings are rich in carbon and lignin. They take many months to break down under the slow action of wood-decaying fungi. They play an important structural role in the earth by preventing compaction and improving the infiltration of rainwater into the deeper soil layers.
common.name: Hazelnut shells

badly damaged: 🔴 Tomato vines reduced to blackened woody filaments and rotten moldy fibers.
🔴 This plant residue is in advanced decay, covered with gray mycelium.
🔴 Tomato vine debris melted into a shapeless, spongy organic mush.
damaged: 🟠 Withered and softened tomato vines, showing brown rot spots along the stems.
🟠 These stems present significant tears and a loss of fiber rigidity.
🟠 Worn plant remains whose green stalks are starting to dry and turn yellow.
worn: 🟡 Dry but whole tomato vines, discarded after harvesting the fruits.
🟡 These green branches have retained their tomato foliage smell and fuzzy texture.
🟡 A batch of clean tomato vines, showing neat shear cuts at the ends.

common.fun_fact: These long, fibrous stems contain cellulose, lignin, and nitrogen. They decompose in a few weeks under the effect of filamentous fungi. Their degradation returns potassium and calcium to the soil, while providing a fibrous structure that maintains terrestrial aeration for optimal microflora growth in the soil.
common.name: Tomato vines

badly damaged: 🔴 A toothbrush with its nylon bristles completely torn off and the handle split.
🔴 This composite plastic handle is broken in two and covered in stubborn limescale.
🔴 A piece of rigid, curved plastic, the remains of a faded brush handle.
damaged: 🟠 A toothbrush with synthetic bristles ruffled and flattened from long use.
🟠 The ergonomic handle shows gray wear marks and tiny surface scratches.
🟠 This oral hygiene utensil displays shallow cracks on its non-slip rubber grip.
worn: 🟡 A used toothbrush still whole, with its bristles slightly bent on the sides.
🟡 This bi-color plastic handle is slightly faded from bathroom humidity.
🟡 A recently discarded toothbrush, its bristles still clean but somewhat worn.

common.fun_fact: The standard toothbrush combines multiple insoluble plastic types, including a rigid polypropylene handle and heat-fused nylon bristles. This intimate fusion of different polymers makes industrial recycling impossible. Over time, it slowly fragments into persistent microplastics that pollute waterways, contaminate groundwater, and accumulate in the tissues of delicate aquatic wildlife.
common.name: Used toothbrush

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of glazed terracotta reduced to a dull, polished pebble-like state.
🔴 This piece of broken dishware is cracked in several places and covered in black silt.
🔴 A ceramic shard whose painted design has become completely invisible due to erosion.
damaged: 🟠 A sharp fragment originating from a white porcelain plate or bowl.
🟠 This piece of earthenware shows a clean break with very sharp edges.
🟠 A piece of glazed ceramic cup, with traces of sediment on the corners.
worn: 🟡 A large piece of stoneware tile with its glossy glaze almost intact.
🟡 This modern pottery shard shows slight scratches on its colored surface.
🟡 A fragment of a decorated cup that still retains its handle despite the fall.

common.fun_fact: Shards of earthenware, porcelain, and stoneware have a melting temperature much higher than standard packaging glass. If mixed with recyclable bottles, they create major structural defects known as unfused stones, making new bottles fragile and highly prone to breaking. Therefore, these inert ceramic fragments must be separated from glass streams and sent to appropriate disposal sites.
common.name: Ceramic shard

badly damaged: 🔴 Small silvered glass shards clumped together in silty, dark sand.
🔴 This mirror fragment has lost almost all of its reflective metallic backing.
🔴 A residue of flat glass, opaque and yellowed by slippery organic deposits.
damaged: 🟠 A mirror sheet broken in a star pattern with deep transverse cracks.
🟠 This reflective glass piece shows sharp splinters and flaking silvering.
🟠 A piece of a broken pocket mirror whose surface is tarnished by silt.
worn: 🟡 A worn but flat piece of mirror that still reflects a clear image underwater.
🟡 This mirror debris displays superficial scratches without major silvering loss.
🟡 A portion of reflective glass with only its corners slightly dulled.

common.fun_fact: The glass used for mirrors is a specific flat glass whose chemical composition differs significantly from standard packaging glass. Additionally, its back is coated with a protective metallic silvering layer that disrupts the operations of recycling furnaces. This composite waste cannot be melted down with ordinary bottles and must be permanently disposed of via residual collection streams.
common.name: Broken mirror

badly damaged: 🔴 A disposable diaper swollen with river water, torn open and leaking its internal gel.
🔴 This hygiene product is half-decomposed, covered in foul-smelling silt.
🔴 Shreds of white cellulose and elastic plastic caught beneath a submerged branch.
damaged: 🟠 A baby diaper saturated with water, its adhesive strips completely torn away.
🟠 This disposable protection shows exposed, soiled synthetic cotton fibers.
🟠 A used diaper that has become heavy after stagnating at the bottom of the water.
worn: 🟡 A whole but wet disposable diaper, recently discarded and carried by currents.
🟡 This infant diaper shows crumpling marks and slight outer discoloration.
🟡 A used diaper discarded intact, still retaining its plastic outer structure.

common.fun_fact: A disposable diaper is a complex assembly of wood pulp cellulose, superabsorbent sodium polyacrylate, and multiple protective polypropylene or polyethylene films. This inseparable mixture of wet organic material and petroleum-based plastics makes mechanical sorting impossible, requiring direct incineration with energy recovery or placement in long-term waste containment facilities.
common.name: Disposable diaper

badly damaged: 🔴 A synthetic wipe frayed into a clump of sticky, gray fibers.
🔴 This used wipe is embedded with black silt and wrapped tightly around a root.
🔴 A piece of torn non-woven fabric, almost translucent under the effect of water.
damaged: 🟠 A cleaning wipe full of holes, having lost its whiteness and artificial scent.
🟠 This disposable fabric shows fraying along the edges and greasy stains.
🟠 A used wet wipe partially deformed by the strength of the river current.
worn: 🟡 A disinfectant wipe discarded whole, still flexible but gray with dust.
🟡 This rectangular piece of non-woven material shows minor wrinkles without tears.
🟡 A worn cleaning wipe washed up on the wet sand of the riverbank.

common.fun_fact: Unlike toilet paper made of pure cellulose, cleaning wipes consist of non-woven synthetic fibers, often polyester or viscose. They do not dissolve in water and create massive blockages in sewerage networks, trapping other harmful materials and blocking pumps at treatment plants before they are eventually screened out and sent to the incinerator for final disposal.
common.name: Disinfectant wipe

badly damaged: 🔴 Softened pieces of brown cardboard, reduced to an oily, dark pulp.
🔴 A shredded cardboard box whose internal surface is black with mold.
🔴 A portion of corrugated cardboard box in advanced decay, stuck to river weeds.
damaged: 🟠 A pizza box warped by water, with large rings of congealed grease.
🟠 This cardboard lid shows major tears and stuck-on cheese residues.
🟠 A cardboard package folded in half, saturated with brackish water and smelling rancid.
worn: 🟡 A whole but flattened pizza box showing cooking oil stains.
🟡 This corrugated cardboard shows slight dampness with its printed logos still legible.
🟡 A recently discarded pizza box, dry on top but stained on the inside.

common.fun_fact: Cardboard soiled by food grease or vegetable oils cannot be recycled by traditional paper mills. The fats do not separate from the cellulose fibers during the pulping process, which spoils the quality of the recycled paper pulp and results in structural holes in the new paper sheets. This dirty cardboard packaging must therefore be disposed of with general non-recyclable household waste.
common.name: Greasy pizza box

badly damaged: 🔴 A pile of clay granules clumped into a foul-smelling, shapeless gray mud.
🔴 This wet mineral litter has dissolved into a sticky sediment at the river bottom.
🔴 Hardened clay blocks mixed with plant debris and dark silt.
damaged: 🟠 A broken clump of clumping clay litter releasing dusty grayish grains.
🟠 This mineral litter shows dried urine stains and emits an ammonia odor.
🟠 Silica granules saturated with water, having completely lost their absorbing power.
worn: 🟡 A handful of recently discarded mineral litter granules, still coarse.
🟡 This pile of absorbent gravel shows minor dampness without dissolving.
🟡 Pet litter composed of small grains of clumped gray bentonite.

common.fun_fact: Mineral pet litter is primarily made of swelling clays like bentonite or silica gel. Once soiled by animal waste, it harbors pathogenic parasites like Toxoplasma gondii. Its inorganic nature combined with its heavy bacterial load prevents any biological processing by composting or standard material recycling, making its disposal in landfill or incineration systems necessary.
common.name: Soiled cat litter

badly damaged: 🔴 A thick paper bag torn open, releasing clumps of compact gray dust.
🔴 This non-woven filter package is ruptured, revealing a mixture of hair and silt.
🔴 Shreds of brown cellulose fibers saturated with a putrid black mud.
damaged: 🟠 A vacuum cleaner bag swollen with water, its cardboard seams peeling apart.
🟠 This filtering envelope shows punctures and is leaking fine indoor dust.
🟠 A dusty collection bag whose rigid plastic collar is broken or warped.
worn: 🟡 A whole vacuum bag discarded full, heavy with compressed domestic debris.
🟡 This multi-layer paper filter pocket shows superficial damp stains.
🟡 A used dust collection bag whose thick cardboard ring is still intact.

common.fun_fact: The vacuum cleaner bag is made of kraft paper lined with non-woven synthetic fibers to trap microparticles, plus a glued plastic or cardboard fixing ring. This multi-material design, combined with the unknown and often toxic nature of the vacuumed household dust, prohibits any recycling of its structure, requiring its disposal along with unsorted residual waste.
common.name: Full Vacuum Cleaner Bag

badly damaged: 🔴 A yellowed, frayed cellulose acetate filter releasing fine synthetic fibers.
🔴 This cigarette remnant is crushed, blackened by water, and half-decomposed.
🔴 A tiny cylinder of synthetic fibers embedded in sticky bottom silt.
damaged: 🟠 A damp cigarette butt with pieces of brown paper wrapping still attached.
🟠 This used filter shows dark tar stains and emits a strong stale tobacco odor.
🟠 A cigarette butt warped by water, its protective paper wrapping unrolling.
worn: 🟡 A recently discarded whole cigarette butt, its orange paper wrapper intact.
🟡 This used filter displays minor fading from sunlight without losing its shape.
🟡 A smoked cigarette thrown into the water, its remaining tobacco now dissolved.

common.fun_fact: A single cigarette butt contains thousands of toxic chemical substances like nicotine, cadmium, and lead trapped in its cellulose acetate filter. This plastic takes over ten years to fragment, polluting up to five hundred liters of water and poisoning delicate aquatic organisms. This hazardous chemical waste must be collected and disposed of permanently via non-recyclable streams.
common.name: Cigarette butt

badly damaged: 🔴 A hardened, black ball of gum embedded with sand grains and tiny gravel.
🔴 This chewing gum is flattened, faded by water, and covered with a film of silt.
🔴 A grayish elastic residue, stuck and fossilized onto a rough river stone.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of soft, deformed chewing gum showing visible tooth impressions.
🟠 This chewed gum has lost its original flavor and shows a dull pink color.
🟠 A sticky chewing gum covered in fine river silt and small algae strands.
worn: 🟡 A recently chewed gum, still flexible and white or green in color.
🟡 This piece of elastic gum shows minor surface wrinkles without embedded debris.
🟡 A worn ball of gum floating between two water layers after being spat out.

common.fun_fact: Modern chewing gum is manufactured from a synthetic gum base composed of elastomers, waxes, and petroleum-derived polymers similar to polyethylene. Completely non-biodegradable and impossible to sort or recycle due to its sticky nature and small size, it must be incinerated with residual waste to prevent long-term accumulation and contamination in the environment.
common.name: Chewed gum

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic pen broken into several pieces, lacking its ink refill and cap.
🔴 This transparent pen body is yellowed by the sun and clogged with mud.
🔴 A split plastic tube whose ink cartridge has leaked into the surrounding water.
damaged: 🟠 A scratched ballpoint pen with its metallic tip oxidized by humidity.
🟠 This writing instrument shows cracked plastic and an outdated ink supply.
🟠 A plastic pen whose push-button mechanism is broken or blocked by sand.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn plastic ballpoint pen, its writing tip still protected.
🟡 This clear pen shows a few superficial scratches on its plastic shirt clip.
🟡 A used pen whose outer plastic is slightly dull but not deformed.

common.fun_fact: The ballpoint pen is a complex assembly of polystyrene for the rigid body, polypropylene for the cap, and a tungsten carbide tip set in brass. Its small size and the presence of hydrophobic grease-based ink residues prevent its detection and processing in standard sorting centers for packaging plastics. This multi-material product must be disposed of with general residual waste.
common.name: Worn ballpoint pen

badly damaged: 🔴 A piece of thermal paper blackened and reduced to shapeless shreds.
🔴 This receipt is almost completely decomposed, forming a sticky gray paste.
🔴 A fragment of thin paper receipt whose ink has washed away in the water.
damaged: 🟠 A crumpled receipt showing large gray spots from thermal chemical reaction.
🟠 This receipt shows significant tears and a texture softened by moisture.
🟠 A store ticket folded in four whose printed text has become illegible.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled store receipt, recently discarded on the wet ground.
🟡 This receipt shows minor folding marks without chemical alteration of the paper.
🟡 A thermal ticket whose printed characters are still partially visible.

common.fun_fact: Receipts are made of thermal paper coated with a chemical dye that reacts to heat. This coating often contains bisphenol A or S, endocrine disruptors that would contaminate the water recycling loop if this paper were processed together with clean paper and cardboard. To protect other paper recycling streams, it must be discarded with general non-recyclable household waste.
common.name: Receipt receipt

badly damaged: 🔴 A deposit of fine ashes diluted in silty water, forming a gray residue.
🔴 This combustion dust has clumped together into a compact alkaline paste.
🔴 Powdery charcoal residues mixed with muddy bottom sediments.
damaged: 🟠 A pile of gray wood ashes still containing small pieces of charred wood.
🟠 These fireplace remains show significant dampness and small hardened lumps.
🟠 Fly ash scattered at the bottom of the water, forming a grayish veil.
worn: 🟡 A batch of dry, powdery chimney ashes, light gray in color.
🟡 These burnt wood residues show fine, unaltered black charcoal debris.
🟡 Recently discarded wood ashes, still light and dry before getting wet.

common.fun_fact: Ashes from wood heating or barbecues concentrate heavy metals naturally absorbed by trees during their growth, along with polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from incomplete combustion. These powdery residues cannot be recycled and must be discarded cold in general household waste to prevent the spread of toxic dust and chemical contamination in water systems.
common.name: Wood ash

badly damaged: 🔴 A shredded kitchen sponge, reduced to a heap of shapeless synthetic foam.
🔴 This cleaning utensil is blackened by mold and encrusted with greasy silt.
🔴 Crumbled pieces of polyurethane foam that disintegrate when touched.
damaged: 🟠 A synthetic sponge with its green abrasive scouring side half torn off.
🟠 This foam block shows major tears and embedded organic debris.
🟠 A used sponge warped by humidity and hardened by crystallized marine salt.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn dishwashing sponge, faded yellow or pink in color.
🟡 This cleaning tool displays minor signs of wear on its abrasive side.
🟡 A recently discarded sponge, still elastic despite a few gray stains.

common.fun_fact: The synthetic kitchen sponge is usually made of polyurethane foam glued to an abrasive scouring web of polyester fibers loaded with phenolic resins. This glued bi-material structure cannot be recycled and ultimately releases toxic microplastics during its environmental degradation. It must be discarded along with general non-recyclable household waste.
common.name: Synthetic sponge

badly damaged: 🔴 A razor handle broken in two, missing its metal head and yellowed by salt.
🔴 This plastic razor shows steel blades that are completely rusted and detached.
🔴 A molded rigid plastic fragment covered in silt and decayed vegetation.
damaged: 🟠 A disposable razor with orange rust forming on its steel blades.
🟠 This blue razor shows a scratched handle and a degraded lubricating strip.
🟠 A used shaving tool whose plastic head is cracked or slightly deformed.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn disposable razor, lacking its clear plastic protective cover.
🟡 This plastic handle shows minor surface scratches without blade corrosion.
🟡 A recently discarded plastic razor, still clean despite a slightly dull finish.

common.fun_fact: The disposable razor is a perfect example of a non-recyclable multi-material product. It combines a rigid polystyrene handle, a lubricating strip of water-soluble polymers, and stainless steel blades. Separating these materials on an industrial scale is economically impossible. Thus, it must be routed to standard household incineration for final disposal.
common.name: Disposable razor

badly damaged: 🔴 A white plastic stick bent at a right angle, lacking cotton tips.
🔴 This cotton swab is flattened, yellowed, and embedded in smelly black silt.
🔴 A frayed rigid plastic stick caught between two small river stones.
damaged: 🟠 A used cotton swab with ruffled, soiled cotton fibers on its ends.
🟠 This plastic stem shows bending stress marks and slight discoloration.
🟠 A damp cotton swab whose body of plastic or compressed paper is softening.
worn: 🟡 A cotton swab discarded whole, its cotton tips still relatively compact.
🟡 This small stick shows minor handling marks without structural failure.
🟡 A white plastic cotton swab whose cotton is slightly gray with dust.

common.fun_fact: Plastic-stemmed cotton swabs were historically one of the most common wastes found in aquatic environments. Too thin to be intercepted by wastewater treatment grids, they pass through filters and float in seas before being ingested by seabirds who mistake these sticks for food. They must be placed in secure household disposal streams.
common.name: Used cotton swab

badly damaged: 🔴 A protective mask in blue shreds, with its elastic loops broken.

damaged: 🟠 A crumpled surgical mask with mud accumulated on its pleated surface.
🟠 This mask shows a bent metal nose wire and a stretched-out elastic loop.
🟠 A used disposable respiratory filter whose blue color has faded in the sun.

🟡 This protective mask shows minor wrinkles without tears on the filter fabric.
🟡 A recently discarded mask whose elastic ear loops are still intact.

common.fun_fact: The disposable surgical mask is made of non-woven polypropylene, a synthetic plastic obtained by melt-blowing. Its structure also integrates a steel nose clip sheathed in plastic and spandex elastics. This mix of metals and polymers makes recycling complex and financially unviable, requiring permanent destruction through thermal waste treatment.
common.name: Mask surgical

badly damaged: 🔴 A fragment of natural vulcanized rubber decomposing beneath riverbed stones.
🔴 This glove is completely torn into strips stuck together by dark silt.
🔴 A piece of hardened and cracked elastic latex, reduced to a yellow skin.
damaged: 🟠 A latex glove with major punctures at the fingertips.
🟠 This protective glove shows traces of black dirt and chemical discoloration.
🟠 An elastic disposable glove stretched out and filled with wet, fine sand.
worn: 🟡 A whole latex glove discarded after use, pale yellow or white in color.
🟡 This hand protection shows minor wrinkles without any visible tears.
🟡 A worn thin rubber glove that still retains its original hand-like shape.

common.fun_fact: Although latex comes from the sap of the rubber tree, disposable gloves undergo sulfur vulcanization and contain chemical additives to improve elasticity. These treatments prevent biological composting or mechanical recycling, requiring thermal incineration alongside general household waste to prevent chemical and biological contamination.
common.name: Latex gloves with holes

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic hairbrush with its handle broken off and all bristles ripped out.
🔴 This brush body is encrusted with dried algae, silt, and tangled hair.
🔴 A molded plastic fragment, the remnant of a hairbrush faded by water exposure.
damaged: 🟠 A hairbrush with bent plastic pins and a cracked rubber cushion base.
🟠 This plastic brush shows deep surface scratches and stubborn limescale build-up.
🟠 A worn hair styling tool whose ergonomic handle is warped or deformed.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn hairbrush, missing only a few plastic bristles.
🟡 This plastic model shows minor scratches on its back without major damage.
🟡 A recently discarded hairbrush still containing a few tangled strands of hair.

common.fun_fact: A hairbrush often combines an ABS rigid plastic body, a flexible elastomer cushion, and nylon or metal bristles tipped with epoxy resin. This intimate mix of glued materials cannot be separated for plastic recycling and must be permanently disposed of in the general household waste streams for mechanical destruction.
common.name: Hairbrush

badly damaged: 🔴 Fine dust residue diluted in muddy water, forming a grayish deposit.
🔴 This dust pile has clumped into a compact paste saturated with organic debris.
🔴 Household sweepings dissolved at the water bottom, forming sterile silt.
damaged: 🟠 A grayish pile of sweepings containing fabric fibers and small breadcrumbs.
🟠 This dust shows large mixed debris and dark clumps of hair.
🟠 Damp domestic cleaning residues, forming soft balls of gray fibers.
worn: 🟡 A dry and powdery pile of sweepings, recently accumulated from floor cleaning.
🟡 This residue shows fine sand grains mixed with fabric lint.
🟡 Dry sweepings discarded in a packet, containing microscopic fibers.

common.fun_fact: Sweeping dust is a concentration of human dander, pet hair, synthetic microfibres from carpets and clothing, soot, and outdoor particles. This heterogeneous and chemical-heavy mixture cannot be recovered by biological composting or mechanical recycling and must be thrown into general household waste to prevent wind dispersal.
common.name: Sweeping dust

badly damaged: 🔴 A clump of hair decomposed into a black, slimy felt covered in silt.
🔴 This pet fur is amalgamated with river bottom clay, forming a sticky mass.
🔴 Frayed capillary fibers embedded in decaying organic debris.
damaged: 🟠 A mass of tangled hair forming a wet ball full of fine sand.
🟠 This dog or cat fur shows residues of dirt and decayed leaves.
🟠 A hair clump twisted by water eddies, holding small pieces of wood.
worn: 🟡 A recently discarded clump of cut hair, still flexible and dry.
🟡 This ball of pet fur displays well-preserved natural color shades.
🟡 A clean capillary cluster floating gently adrift between water layers.

common.fun_fact: Although made of biodegradable keratin, human hair and pet fur collected in the domestic environment are often contaminated with chemical cosmetics, silicones, and shampoo residues. When mixed with household waste, they must be disposed of via standard non-recyclable streams to avoid contaminating organic composting cycles.
common.name: Hair

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of flowerpot eroded into a dull, orange clay pebble.
🔴 This piece of terracotta is cracked in several places and covered in silt.
🔴 A porous pottery fragment encrusted with green algae and white limescale.
damaged: 🟠 A sharp fragment originating from a red clay flowerpot.
🟠 This terracotta piece shows a break with sharp angles and stuck-on potting soil.
🟠 The base of an unglazed ceramic pot, displaying mineral salt deposits.
worn: 🟡 A large piece of flowerpot with its curved rim still recognizable.
🟡 This terracotta shard shows minor soil traces without surface erosion.
🟡 A recently broken garden pottery fragment, bright orange in color.

common.fun_fact: Terracotta is clay baked at high temperatures that has lost its ability to become malleable again in water. This inert material cannot be recycled with glass packaging due to its high melting point, nor blended with plastics, requiring it to be disposed of as residual waste in appropriate containment facilities.
common.name: Broken terracotta pot

badly damaged: 🔴 A shard of white tableware reduced to a small, dull, polished pebble.
🔴 This plate fragment has network cracks and is covered in black mud.
🔴 A piece of porcelain whose decorative gold trim has completely vanished in water.
damaged: 🟠 A piece of a broken plate with edges that are sharp like glass.
🟠 This cup debris shows significant cracks across its glazed surface.
🟠 A porcelain shard showing floral patterns that are partially worn away.
worn: 🟡 A recently broken piece of tableware, its white surface still very shiny.
🟡 This saucer debris shows minor sand rubbing marks on its top glaze.
🟡 A fragment of a porcelain plate, clean but broken along its edges.

common.fun_fact: Porcelain is a fine ceramic obtained from kaolin fired at over fourteen hundred degrees. This vitrified material has a highly stable crystalline structure that does not melt in traditional glass-making furnaces. It constitutes a major pollutant for the glass recycling industry and must be thrown in with general non-recyclable rubbish.
common.name: Porcelain tableware

badly damaged: 🔴 A makeup remover pad frayed into a clump of sticky white fibers.
🔴 This used cotton is embedded with black silt and smells of decomposition.
🔴 A torn cotton fiber residue, almost shapeless after a long stay in the water.
damaged: 🟠 A used makeup remover pad showing dark makeup stains and product residues.
🟠 This cotton disc displays stretched fibers and accumulated dirt particles.
🟠 A piece of hydrophilic cotton saturated with gray river water.
worn: 🟡 A whole but damp makeup remover cotton pad, discarded after single use.
🟡 This piece of cotton shows minor surface wrinkles without edge tears.
🟡 A worn cosmetic cotton pad washed up on the wet sand near the water.

common.fun_fact: Although cotton is a natural plant fiber, used makeup remover pads contain residues of chemical cosmetics, solvents, heavy metals from makeup, and microplastics. Furthermore, they are often chlorine-bleached, which excludes them from organic composting options and requires secure disposal in the general non-recyclable waste stream.
common.name: Make-up remover cotton

badly damaged: 🔴 A shriveled ball of plastic adhesive tape, blackened by stuck-on dust.
🔴 This tape is shredded into tiny curled-up pieces that cling to each other.
🔴 A fragment of adhesive plastic film permanently stuck to a piece of driftwood.
damaged: 🟠 A dirty piece of adhesive tape with its glue layer full of sand grains.
🟠 This tape shows tears and has completely lost its original transparency.
🟠 A used adhesive strip displaying major crumpling and dark stains.
worn: 🟡 A whole but worn strip of adhesive tape, recently discarded.
🟡 This self-adhesive plastic shows a slight loss of stickiness without tears.
🟡 A piece of clear tape, crumpled and damp, floating in the water.

common.fun_fact: Standard adhesive tape consists of a backing made of polypropylene or PVC coated with a thermoplastic adhesive based on synthetic rubber and tackifying resins. This blend of solvents and thin plastics makes it incompatible with plastic or cardboard recycling loops, requiring it to be thrown out with general non-recyclable refuse.
common.name: Worn duct tape

badly damaged: 🔴 A brown baking sheet in softened pieces, blackened by burns and oil.
🔴 This parchment paper is torn and covered in black food molds.
🔴 A sticky baking paper residue in advanced decomposition under the silt.
damaged: 🟠 A baking paper warped by moisture with stains from baked-on grease.
🟠 This paper shows charred edges and burnt dough residues stuck to it.
🟠 A parchment sheet folded into a ball, full of river water and smelling rancid.
worn: 🟡 A whole but crumpled sheet of parchment paper, stained with yellow grease.
🟡 This baking paper shows heavy fold lines without any major tearing.
🟡 A worn parchment paper discarded recently, dry on one side and damp on the other.

common.fun_fact: Parchment paper, or baking paper, is treated with sulfuric acid for water resistance and coated with a thin layer of silicone to make it non-stick. This silicone polymer impregnation prevents the wood fibers from dissolving during standard paper recycling processes, requiring it to be placed in general household waste.
common.name: Worn parchment paper

badly damaged: 🔴 A plastic straw split lengthwise, flattened and bleached by marine salt.
🔴 This flexible tube is broken into several segments clogged with black silt.
🔴 A thin cylindrical plastic piece, the remnant of a faded drinking straw.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic straw scratched all over, with its accordion elbow torn or warped.

worn: 🟡 A whole but worn red or blue plastic straw, recently discarded.
🟡 This plastic tube shows superficial scratches without major warping.
🟡 A used straw whose rigid structure is preserved despite floating in the water.

common.fun_fact: Standard plastic straws are made of polypropylene. Because of their hollow cylindrical shape and low weight, they often escape automatic optical sorting systems. They represent a direct threat to aquatic wildlife, which can ingest them by accident. They must be disposed of with general non-recyclable household rubbish.
common.name: Plastic straw

badly damaged: 🔴 A mass of cling wrap crumpled into a tight ball, black with mud and mold.

🔴 Flexible plastic filaments wrapped around roots and embedded with silt.
damaged: 🟠 A plastic cling wrap showing tears and stuck-on dried food residues.
🟠 This thin envelope shows greasy gray stains and trapped condensation.

🟡 This flexible plastic shows minor dampness without major alteration.
🟡 A worn food wrap floating like a transparent membrane between water layers.

common.fun_fact: Plastic food cling wrap is made of low-density polyethylene or flexible PVC. Its extreme thinness causes it to wrap around drive shafts and rotors in sorting centers, leading to costly mechanical breakdowns. Therefore, it must be excluded from standard packaging recycling and thrown into the non-recyclable garbage.
common.name: Soiled cling film

badly damaged: 🔴 A metallized candy wrapper torn into tiny, faded plastic shreds.

🔴 A piece of shiny plastic film whose printed logos have washed off in water.

🟠 This shiny plastic shows dried sugar spots and accumulated dust.

🟡 This sachet shows minor superficial wrinkles without any major holes.

common.fun_fact: Confectionery wrappers are often made of complex films integrating polypropylene and a thin layer of metallized aluminum to protect the candy from moisture. This type of multi-layer composite plastic film cannot be separated industrially for recycling purposes and must be placed in general non-recyclable household waste.

Although entirely plastic, their small cylindrical shape prevents them from being captured by automated sorting centers for standard household packaging. They must be thrown away with general unsorted household refuse.

This combination of synthetic materials, metals, and insoluble adhesive agents prevents any material recycling or composting. It must be discarded with general non-recyclable household rubbish.

These high-tensile technical plastics do not degrade and easily wrap around the fins of aquatic animals, causing severe injuries. They must be safely thrown into the non-recyclable garbage.

common.fun_fact: Glossy photo paper is coated with a receptive layer of plastic resins like polyethylene, and sometimes metallic oxides to fix inks. This composite plastic-paper formulation prevents the recovery of cellulose fibers during standard paper recycling processes, requiring its disposal along with unsorted residual waste.
common.name: Glossy photo paper

Additionally, it is saturated with water-insoluble methylcellulose adhesive residues. These additives prevent it from being recycled alongside clean papers and require it to be thrown out with general waste.

Additionally, when soiled with biological fluids, they pose a biohazard that excludes them from recycling loops alongside clean packaging cardboard. They must be discarded with general household refuse.

This dense calcareous material does not melt during glass recycling and must be incinerated or discarded with general residual waste to prevent damage to recycling machinery.

common.fun_fact: The charcoal bag is made of heavy multi-ply kraft paper, often glued with synthetic adhesives and blackened by pure carbon dust. These carbon residues spoil the paper pulp during recycling, making its disposal via standard household waste streams necessary to protect paper reclamation facilities and keep municipal processing smooth.
